topics.title: RC Coupled CE Amplifier: Frequency Response Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** RC Coupled CE Amplifier: Frequency Response Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **RC Coupled Common Emitter (CE) Amplifier** is a small-signal voltage amplifier in which the input and output ports are interconnected through **resistor–capacitor (RC) coupling networks**. The active device is a bipolar junction transistor (BJT) configured in the common-emitter mode, biased in the active region, with the emitter resistor either partially or fully bypassed by a large electrolytic capacitor. The "frequency response" denotes the manner in which the **magnitude of the mid-band voltage gain** varies as a function of the **sinusoidal input signal frequency**, plotted conventionally on a semi-logarithmic (Bode) scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The frequency response is divided into three distinct regions — **Low-Frequency Region**, **Mid-Frequency Region (Flat Band)**, and **High-Frequency Region**. The laboratory objective is to plot $\vert A_V \vert$ versus frequency and determine the **lower cutoff frequency $f_1$**, **upper cutoff frequency $f_2$**, **bandwidth (BW)**, and **gain bandwidth product (GBW)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Three-Speed Ceiling Fan"

Think of the RC coupled CE amplifier as a **three-speed ceiling fan** controlled by a regulator:

| Speed Region | Fan Analogy | Amplifier Equivalent |
|--------------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Low Speed (Regulator at Min)** | Fan runs slowly because the heavy capacitor takes time to "charge up" the windings | Gain falls because coupling capacitor $C_1, C_2$ and emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$ behave like **high impedances** at low frequencies |
| **Medium Speed (Regulator Mid)** | Fan runs at full, smooth speed — the motor reaches its rated RPM | Gain is **constant and maximum** — internal reactances are negligible, only resistors dictate the gain |
| **High Speed (Regulator at Max — Turbo kicks in)** | Fan blades vibrate and shake due to mechanical inertia limits | Gain falls because **internal transistor capacitances** ($C_{be}$, $C_{bc}$) shunt high frequencies to ground |

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard $3\text{ dB}$ Down Definition:** The cutoff frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ are defined as the points where the output power drops to **one-half** of the mid-band power, which corresponds to the voltage gain falling to **$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$** of its mid-band value.
- **Reference Frequency:** **$1\text{ kHz}$** is universally used as the mid-band reference in KTU lab experiments.
- **Gain in Decibels:** $A_V(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$ — measured in **decibels (dB)**, a unitless logarithmic ratio.
- **Standard Power Supply:** $V_{CC} = +12\text{ V}$ (KTU lab convention).
- **Standard Transistor:** **BC107 / SL100 / 2N2222** (silicon NPN, $h_{fe} \approx 100$–$300$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Note:** The bandwidth of an audio amplifier (human hearing range **$20\text{ Hz}$–$20\text{ kHz}$**) is the principal reason why **RC coupling** dominates audio pre-amplifier design — its bandwidth naturally spans the audible spectrum.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Frequency Regions — A Step-by-Step Decomposition

The frequency response curve of an RC coupled CE amplifier is the **product of two transfer functions**: a **high-pass response** generated by the external RC coupling network, and a **low-pass response** generated by the internal transistor capacitances. Mathematically, the overall normalized gain is:

$$\dfrac{A_V(f)}{A_{V(\text{mid})}} = \underbrace{\dfrac{j(f/f_1)}{\sqrt{1+(f/f_1)^2}}}_{\text{Low-Frequency Rise}} \times \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+(f/f_2)^2}}}_{\text{High-Frequency Roll-Off}}$$

### Region 1 — Low-Frequency Region ($f < f_1$)

- **Cause:** The reactance of the **input coupling capacitor $C_1$**, **output coupling capacitor $C_2$**, and the **emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$** becomes significant.
- **Physical Reason:** $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ — at low $f$, $X_C$ is very large, dropping a substantial portion of the input signal across the capacitor.
- **Slope:** Gain rises at **$+20\text{ dB/decade}$** as frequency increases.
- **Key Capacitors Responsible:** $C_1, C_2, C_E$.

### Region 2 — Mid-Frequency Region ($f_1 \le f \le f_2$)

- **Cause:** All external coupling and bypass capacitors behave as **AC short circuits** ($X_C \approx 0$), and all internal transistor capacitances behave as **AC open circuits** ($X_C \approx \infty$).
- **Physical Reason:** The circuit reduces to a **purely resistive small-signal equivalent**, making the gain **independent of frequency**.
- **Slope:** **$0\text{ dB/decade}$** — flat plateau.
- **Mid-Band Voltage Gain:** $A_{V(\text{mid})} = -\dfrac{h_{fe} \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)}{h_{ie}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative sign in the mid-band gain indicates a **$180^\circ$ phase inversion** between input and output — a defining hallmark of the common-emitter configuration.

### Region 3 — High-Frequency Region ($f > f_2$)

- **Cause:** The **internal junction capacitances** of the BJT — **base-emitter diffusion capacitance $C_{be}$** and **base-collector depletion capacitance $C_{bc}$** — begin to conduct.
- **Physical Reason:** The base-collector capacitance $C_{bc}$ introduces the **Miller Effect**, multiplying its effective input capacitance by the voltage gain plus one.
- **Slope:** Gain falls at **$-20\text{ dB/decade}$** as frequency increases.

## 2.2 Why Each Capacitor Acts the Way It Does

| Capacitor | Physical Location | Role at Low $f$ | Role at Mid $f$ | Role at High $f$ |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|------------------|------------------|
| $C_1$ (Input Coupling) | Between source and base | High $X_C$ → blocks DC, attenuates signal | Short circuit → passes signal | Short circuit |
| $C_2$ (Output Coupling) | Between collector and load | High $X_C$ → attenuates output | Short circuit → passes amplified signal | Short circuit |
| $C_E$ (Emitter Bypass) | Across $R_E$ | High $X_C$ → $R_E$ un-bypassed, gain drops | Short circuit → $R_E$ fully bypassed | Short circuit |
| $C_{be}$ (Internal) | B-E junction | Open | Open | Low $X_C$ → shunts signal to emitter |
| $C_{bc}$ (Internal) | B-C junction (Miller) | Open | Open | Low $X_C$ → feedback path, shunts high $f$ to ground |

## 2.3 The Miller Effect — The Hidden Multiplier

The base-collector capacitance $C_{bc}$ appears between input and output. Because the output voltage is **$A_V$ times larger** than the input, the current through $C_{bc}$ is amplified, which is equivalent to placing a much larger capacitor across the input terminals. This is **Miller's Theorem**:

$$C_{M} = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_V \vert)$$

The total input capacitance becomes $C_{in(\text{total})} = C_{be} + C_M$, which is the principal cause of high-frequency gain roll-off.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** Vertical pipes $\vert$ inside table cells are replaced with $\vert$ LaTeX command to prevent markdown table syntax breakage.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | Mid-band voltage gain (magnitude) | $\vert A_{V(\text{mid})} \vert = \dfrac{h_{fe} \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)}{h_{ie}}$ | Unitless | Measured at $1\text{ kHz}$ reference |
| 2 | Gain in decibels | $A_V(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\vert A_V \vert\right)$ | dB | Standard logarithmic form |
| 3 | Lower cutoff from input coupling $C_1$ | $f_{1C_1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S \parallel h_{ie}) C_1}$ | Hz | Dominates if $C_1$ is small |
| 4 | Lower cutoff from output coupling $C_2$ | $f_{1C_2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_2}$ | Hz | Usually high, rarely dominant |
| 5 | Lower cutoff from bypass $C_E$ | $f_{1C_E} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R'_E \, C_E}$ | Hz | $R'_E = R_E \parallel \left[\dfrac{R_S' + h_{ie}}{1 + h_{fe}}\right]$ |
| 6 | Overall lower cutoff (dominant pole) | $f_1 \approx \max(f_{1C_1}, f_{1C_2}, f_{1C_E})$ | Hz | Highest of the three dominates |
| 7 | Upper cutoff frequency | $f_2 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R'_T \cdot C_{in(\text{total})}}$ | Hz | $R'_T = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel h_{ie}$ |
| 8 | Total input capacitance | $C_{in(\text{total})} = C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_V \vert)$ | F | Miller's theorem applied |
| 9 | Bandwidth | $\text{BW} = f_2 - f_1$ | Hz | Useful amplification range |
| 10 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $\text{GBW} = \vert A_{V(\text{mid})} \vert \cdot \text{BW}$ | Hz | Constant for a given transistor |
| 11 | 3 dB point gain ratio | $\dfrac{A_V(f_{1,2})}{A_{V(\text{mid})}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ | Unitless | Definition of cutoff |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The frequency response of an RC coupled amplifier is the cornerstone of **audio engineering**, **communication receivers**, and **operational amplifier input stages**. For example:
- A **public address (PA) system** requires a bandwidth of at least **$50\text{ Hz}$–$15\text{ kHz}$** to faithfully reproduce voice and music.
- The **Miller Effect** dictates the upper frequency limit of every integrated BJT amplifier — a constraint that drove the invention of **cascode amplifiers** and **FET input stages** in modern op-amps.
- The **emitter bypass capacitor** is the single most critical component in determining $f_1$ in a poorly designed lab circuit.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mid-Band Voltage Gain $A_{V(\text{mid})}$

**Step 1 — Replace the transistor with its small-signal hybrid-$\pi$ model.**

At mid-band, all capacitors are short or open, leaving a purely resistive circuit. The small-signal model gives an input loop containing $h_{ie}$ and a current source $h_{fe} \cdot I_b$ at the output.

**Step 2 — Write the input loop equation (KVL).**

The input voltage $V_{in}$ drives current $I_b$ through the input resistance $h_{ie}$:

$$V_{in} = I_b \cdot h_{ie}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the output load.**

The output current $h_{fe} \cdot I_b$ flows through the parallel combination of the collector resistor $R_C$ and the load resistor $R_L$:

$$V_{out} = -h_{fe} \cdot I_b \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

The negative sign arises because an increase in $I_b$ reduces the collector voltage (inversion).

**Step 4 — Divide $V_{out}$ by $V_{in}$ to obtain voltage gain.**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{V(\text{mid})} &= \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{-h_{fe} \cdot I_b \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)}{I_b \cdot h_{ie}} \\[6pt]
&= -\dfrac{h_{fe} \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)}{h_{ie}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — State the magnitude.**

$$\vert A_{V(\text{mid})} \vert = \dfrac{h_{fe} \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)}{h_{ie}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For typical lab values $h_{fe} = 200$, $h_{ie} = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_C = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$:
> $R_C \parallel R_L = 2.35\text{ k}\Omega$, yielding $\vert A_{V(\text{mid})} \vert = 200 \times 2.35 = 470$, i.e., $\approx 53.4\text{ dB}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lower Cutoff Frequency from $C_1$

**Step 1 — Replace $C_1$ with its impedance $Z_{C_1} = \dfrac{1}{j 2\pi f C_1}$.**

**Step 2 — Identify the Thevenin resistance seen by $C_1$.**

From the input side, $C_1$ sees the source resistance $R_S$ in series with the parallel combination of the bias network $R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2$ and the input resistance of the transistor $h_{ie}$:

$$R_{C_1} = R_S + (R_B \parallel h_{ie})$$

When $R_S \ll R_B \parallel h_{ie}$ (voltage source has low output impedance), $R_{C_1} \approx R_S \parallel h_{ie}$.

**Step 3 — Form the high-pass transfer function.**

$$\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_S} = \dfrac{R_B \parallel h_{ie}}{R_S + (R_B \parallel h_{ie}) + \dfrac{1}{j 2\pi f C_1}}$$

**Step 4 — Set $\left\vert \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_S} \right\vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ to find $f_1$.**

At the cutoff, the imaginary and real parts of the denominator are equal in magnitude:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{2\pi f_1 C_1} &= R_S \parallel h_{ie} \\[6pt]
\therefore f_1 &= \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S \parallel h_{ie}) C_1}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lower Cutoff Frequency from Bypass Capacitor $C_E$

**Step 1 — Reflect the emitter resistance into the base loop.**

A resistance in the emitter appears at the base as $(1 + h_{fe})$ times larger:

$$R'_E = R_E \parallel \left[\dfrac{R_S' + h_{ie}}{1 + h_{fe}}\right]$$

where $R_S' = R_S \parallel R_B$.

**Step 2 — Apply the same high-pass cutoff condition as Derivation 2.**

$$f_{1C_E} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R'_E \, C_E}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the dominant $f_1$.**

The overall $f_1$ is determined by the **largest** of the three individual cutoffs because the cumulative $-3\text{ dB}$ response occurs at the lowest frequency where any single capacitor has its high-pass knee:

$$f_1 = \max(f_{1C_1}, f_{1C_2}, f_{1C_E})$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Upper Cutoff Frequency $f_2$ (Miller Effect)

**Step 1 — Apply Miller's theorem to $C_{bc}$.**

The voltage gain from base to collector is approximately $A_V \approx -g_m R'_C$ (using $g_m = \dfrac{h_{fe}}{h_{ie}}$). The Miller-multiplied input capacitance is:

$$C_{M} = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_V \vert) = C_{bc}(1 + g_m R'_C)$$

**Step 2 — Add the base-emitter capacitance in parallel.**

$$C_{in(\text{total})} = C_{be} + C_{M}$$

**Step 3 — Form the Thevenin resistance at the base node.**

The base node sees the source resistance, the bias network, and $h_{ie}$ all in parallel:

$$R'_T = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel h_{ie}$$

**Step 4 — Combine into a low-pass RC time constant.**

$$f_2 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R'_T \cdot C_{in(\text{total})}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (typical BJT at $I_C = 1\text{ mA}$).**

- $C_{be} = 25\text{ pF}$, $C_{bc} = 5\text{ pF}$, $g_m = 40\text{ mA/V}$, $R'_C = 2.35\text{ k}\Omega$, $R'_T = 1\text{ k}\Omega$.
- $C_{M} = 5\text{ pF} \times (1 + 40 \times 2.35) = 5\text{ pF} \times 95 = 475\text{ pF}$.
- $C_{in(\text{total})} = 25 + 475 = 500\text{ pF}$.
- $f_2 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 500 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 318\text{ kHz}$.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Bandwidth and Gain-Bandwidth Product

**Step 1 — Bandwidth definition.**

$$\text{BW} = f_2 - f_1 \approx f_2 \quad (\text{since } f_2 \gg f_1)$$

**Step 2 — Gain-Bandwidth Product definition.**

$$\text{GBW} = \vert A_{V(\text{mid})} \vert \cdot \text{BW}$$

**Step 3 — Practical numerical illustration.**

Using the example above with $\vert A_{V(\text{mid})} \vert = 470$ and $f_2 = 318\text{ kHz}$:

$$\text{GBW} = 470 \times 318{,}000 \approx 1.49 \times 10^{8}\text{ Hz} = 149\text{ MHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The GBW of a single BJT stage is finite. To extend bandwidth, designers cascade multiple stages, each with a lower gain but a wider bandwidth — a technique known as **bandwidth extension through cascaded gain stages**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow (Frequency Response Engine)

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputSignal[Sinusoidal Input Signal Vi] --> inputCouplingC1[Input Coupling Capacitor C1]
    inputCouplingC1 --> biasNetwork[Bias Network R1 R2]
    biasNetwork --> bjtBase[BJT Base Terminal]
    bjtBase --> bjtActive[BJT in Active Region VCE VCC by 2]
    bjtActive --> collectorNode[Collector Node]
    collectorNode --> outputCouplingC2[Output Coupling Capacitor C2]
    outputCouplingC2 --> loadResistor[Load Resistor RL]
    loadResistor --> outputSignal[Output Signal Vo]
    
    bjtBase --> bypassCapCE[Emitter Bypass Capacitor CE]
    bypassCapCE --> emitterNode[Emitter Node RE]
    emitterNode --> groundRef[Ground Reference]
    
    bjtActive --> millerCap[Miller Capacitance Cbc times 1 plus Av]
    millerCap --> bjtBase
    
    lowFreqRegion[Low Frequency Region f less than f1] --> c1Effect[C1 and C2 and CE act as high impedance]
    midFreqRegion[Mid Frequency Region f1 to f2] --> flatGain[Gain is constant at Av mid]
    highFreqRegion[High Frequency Region f greater than f2] --> millerEffect[Miller and Cbe shunt high frequencies]
    
    inputSignal --> regionSelector{Region of Operation}
    regionSelector --> lowFreqRegion
    regionSelector --> midFreqRegion
    regionSelector --> highFreqRegion
    
    c1Effect --> outputSignal
    flatGain --> outputSignal
    millerEffect --> outputSignal
```

## 4.2 Frequency Response Curve — Schematic ASCII Visualization

The semi-log plot of $\vert A_V \vert$ (in dB) versus $\log_{10}(f)$ has three characteristic zones:

```
   Gain (dB)
     │
 A_vmid ──────────────────────────╮
   (dB)                           │  FLAT MID-BAND
     │                            │
     │ ╲                          │
     │  ╲   -20 dB/decade         │╲  -20 dB/decade
     │   ╲ (High-f Roll-off)      │ ╲
     │    ╲                       │  ╲
     │     ╲                      │   ╲
   -3 ─┼─────╲─────────────────────┼────╲──────  -3 dB Line
     │      ╲                     │     ╲
     │       ╲                    │      ╲
     │        ╲                   │       ╲
     │  +20 dB ╲                  │        ╲
     │  /decade ╲                 │         ╲
     │           ╲                │          ╲
     │            ╲               │           ╲
     └─────────────┴───────────────┴────────────┴──────►
                f1 (Lower)        f_mid=1kHz   f2 (Upper)
                                   (Reference)
```

## 4.3 Pole-Zero Pattern of the Amplifier

```mermaid
graph LR
    zeroAtOrigin[Zero at Origin from Coupling Caps]
    poleAtF1[Pole at f1 from C1 and C2 and CE High Pass Corner]
    poleAtF2[Pole at f2 from Miller and Cbe Low Pass Corner]
    
    zeroAtOrigin -- Magnitude Rises at 20 dB per decade --> poleAtF1
    poleAtF1 -- Flat Plateau --> poleAtF2
    poleAtF2 -- Roll Off at minus 20 dB per decade --> infiniteAxis[Frequency tending to Infinity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The transfer function exhibits one **zero at the origin** (characteristic of high-pass coupling networks), one **real pole at $f_1$**, and one **real pole at $f_2$**. This is the canonical **band-pass shape** of every RC coupled amplifier.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A, Q1(a)]**

**Q1. Define the term "frequency response" of an RC coupled CE amplifier. Why is the response plotted on a semi-log scale?**

**Model Answer:**

The frequency response of an RC coupled CE amplifier is a graph showing the variation of the magnitude of the voltage gain $\vert A_V \vert$ as a function of the input signal frequency $f$, typically expressed in decibels. A semi-log scale is used because the frequency range spans from a few hertz to several hundred kilohertz (4–5 decades), which cannot be represented conveniently on a linear axis. The semi-log plot produces straight-line asymptotes with slopes of $\pm 20\text{ dB/decade}$, making the cutoff frequencies easy to identify graphically. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1(b)]**

**Q2. State the significance of the $-3\text{ dB}$ frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ in the context of an RC coupled amplifier.**

**Model Answer:**

The frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ are the **lower and upper cutoff frequencies**, defined as the points where the voltage gain falls to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ of its mid-band value $A_{V(\text{mid})}$. Equivalently, this corresponds to a power drop of one-half, or a **$3\text{ dB}$ attenuation** relative to mid-band. The difference $\text{BW} = f_2 - f_1$ defines the **useful bandwidth** of the amplifier, i.e., the range of frequencies over which the amplifier provides near-constant gain suitable for faithful signal amplification. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q2]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **mid-band voltage gain** of an RC coupled CE amplifier using the small-signal hybrid model. State clearly the role of the emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$ in this derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An RC coupled CE amplifier uses a BC107 transistor with $h_{ie} = 1.2\text{ k}\Omega$, $h_{fe} = 220$, $R_C = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate the **mid-band voltage gain** in magnitude and in decibels. **(7 marks)**

### Question A — Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** At mid-band, the coupling capacitors $C_1, C_2$ act as short circuits, and the bypass capacitor $C_E$ shorts the emitter resistor $R_E$ to ground. The small-signal AC equivalent uses the **approximate hybrid model** with input resistance $h_{ie}$ and current source $h_{fe} I_b$ at the output. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Apply KVL to the input loop:

$$V_{in} = I_b \cdot h_{ie}$$

This is the input voltage seen at the base. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Apply KCL at the output node. The output current $h_{fe} I_b$ flows through the AC load $R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L$:

$$V_{out} = -h_{fe} I_b (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

The negative sign indicates a $180^\circ$ phase inversion between $V_{out}$ and $V_{in}$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4:** Divide:

$$A_{V(\text{mid})} = -\dfrac{h_{fe}(R_C \parallel R_L)}{h_{ie}}$$

**Role of $C_E$:** $C_E$ provides a low-impedance path to ground for AC signals, ensuring that the emitter is effectively at AC ground potential. Without $C_E$, the AC emitter current would develop a voltage across $R_E$, providing **negative feedback** that drastically reduces the voltage gain. With $C_E$ in place, the mid-band gain reaches its maximum theoretical value. **(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute the AC load resistance:

$$R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L = \dfrac{4.7 \times 4.7}{4.7 + 4.7} = 2.35\text{ k}\Omega \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the gain formula:

$$\vert A_{V(\text{mid})} \vert = \dfrac{h_{fe} \cdot R_{ac}}{h_{ie}} = \dfrac{220 \times 2350}{1200} = 430.83 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to decibels:

$$A_V(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(430.83) = 20 \times 2.6343 = 52.69\text{ dB} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4:** Final result with proper units and interpretation:

$$\boxed{\vert A_{V(\text{mid})} \vert \approx 431 \quad \text{or} \quad 52.7\text{ dB}} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **frequency response curve** of an RC coupled CE amplifier. Label the three regions and the $-3\text{ dB}$ points. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier has a mid-band gain of $60\text{ dB}$ and a lower cutoff frequency of $50\text{ Hz}$. If the upper cutoff frequency is $250\text{ kHz}$, calculate the **bandwidth** and the **gain-bandwidth product (GBW)**. **(7 marks)**

### Question B — Model Solution

**Part (a) — Frequency Response Sketch and Explanation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Draw the standard semi-log plot with $\log_{10}(f)$ on the horizontal axis and $\vert A_V \vert$ in dB on the vertical axis. Mark the reference frequency ($1\text{ kHz}$) at the centre of the mid-band plateau. **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Low-Frequency Region ($f < f_1$):** The gain rises with a slope of $+20\text{ dB/decade}$ as frequency increases. This is caused by the **reactance of $C_1, C_2, C_E$** decreasing with frequency, allowing more signal to pass through. At very low $f$, $X_C$ is large, attenuating the signal. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Mid-Frequency Region ($f_1 \le f \le f_2$):** The gain is **constant and equal to $A_{V(\text{mid})}$**. All capacitors are either shorts ($C_1, C_2, C_E$) or opens ($C_{be}, C_{bc}$). The circuit is purely resistive, so the gain is independent of frequency. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — High-Frequency Region ($f > f_2$):** The gain falls with a slope of $-20\text{ dB/decade}$. Internal transistor capacitances $C_{be}$ and the **Miller-multiplied $C_{bc}$** become low impedance, shunting high-frequency signals to ground. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — $-3\text{ dB}$ Points:** Mark $f_1$ (lower) and $f_2$ (upper) on the curve at the intersections of the asymptotes and the mid-band level, offset by $-3\text{ dB}$. The bandwidth $\text{BW} = f_2 - f_1$. **(1 mark)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert mid-band gain from dB to linear scale:

$$60\text{ dB} = 20 \log_{10}(\vert A_V \vert) \implies \vert A_V \vert = 10^{60/20} = 10^3 = 1000 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the bandwidth:

$$\text{BW} = f_2 - f_1 = 250{,}000 - 50 = 249{,}950\text{ Hz} \approx 250\text{ kHz} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the gain-bandwidth product:

$$\text{GBW} = \vert A_V \vert \times \text{BW} = 1000 \times 249{,}950 \approx 2.5 \times 10^{8}\text{ Hz} = 250\text{ MHz} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4:** Final result:

$$\boxed{\text{BW} \approx 250\text{ kHz}, \quad \text{GBW} \approx 250\text{ MHz}} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $A_{V(\text{mid})}$ — examiners specifically test whether you know that CE configuration **inverts the phase**. Loss: up to **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing $f_1$ and $f_2$** — students often swap the lower and upper cutoff frequencies when calculating bandwidth. Always remember: $f_1 < f_2$ and BW $= f_2 - f_1$.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply Miller's theorem** at $f_2$ — the upper cutoff depends on $C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_V \vert)$, not just $C_{bc}$. Loss: up to **2 marks** in derivation questions.
> 4. **Unit errors in dB conversion** — writing $20 \log$ when the quantity is a power ratio (should be $10 \log$) loses marks. Voltage gains always use $20 \log_{10}$.
> 5. **Not specifying the reference frequency** — KTU labs use $1\text{ kHz}$ as the standard mid-band reference. Failing to mention this in lab viva loses **viva marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam**

- **RC Coupled CE Amplifier** = BJT in CE mode + $C_1, C_2$ coupling + $C_E$ bypass.
- **Three frequency regions:** Low ($+20\text{ dB/dec}$), Mid ($0\text{ dB/dec}$), High ($-20\text{ dB/dec}$).
- **Mid-band gain formula:** $\vert A_{V(\text{mid})} \vert = \dfrac{h_{fe} \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)}{h_{ie}}$ — **always negative** in CE mode.
- **Gain in dB:** $A_V(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(\vert A_V \vert)$ — voltage gains use the **factor of 20**.
- **$-3\text{ dB}$ point** = voltage ratio of $0.707$ = power ratio of $0.5$ = the cutoff frequency.
- **Lower cutoff $f_1$** is dominated by the **bypass capacitor $C_E$** in most lab circuits.
- **Upper cutoff $f_2$** is dominated by the **Miller effect** on $C_{bc}$.
- **Miller's theorem:** $C_{M} = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_V \vert)$ — multiplies $C_{bc}$ by approximately the gain.
- **Bandwidth formula:** $\text{BW} = f_2 - f_1 \approx f_2$ (since $f_2 \gg f_1$).
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product:** $\text{GBW} = \vert A_V \vert \times \text{BW}$ is **constant** for a given transistor.
- **Lab reference frequency** = $1\text{ kHz}$ — use this when plotting the response.
- **Decade rule:** A $20\text{ dB}$ drop per decade = a $10\times$ drop in voltage per $10\times$ rise in frequency.
- **Phase inversion:** $180^\circ$ between $V_{in}$ and $V_{out}$ at mid-band — verify on CRO during the lab.
- **Audio bandwidth:** Human hearing range $20\text{ Hz}$–$20\text{ kHz}$ — design $f_1 < 20\text{ Hz}$ and $f_2 > 20\text{ kHz}$ for audio amplifiers.
- **Standard lab components:** $V_{CC} = +12\text{ V}$, transistor BC107/2N2222, electrolytic $C_E = 100\text{ }\mu\text{F}$, ceramic/polyester $C_1 = C_2 = 10\text{ }\mu\text{F}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MOSFET Amplifier (CS): Frequency Response Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** MOSFET Amplifier (CS): Frequency Response Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Common Source (CS) MOSFET Amplifier** is a small-signal voltage amplifier configuration in which the input signal is applied between the **Gate** and **Source** terminals, the output is taken between the **Drain** and **Source** terminals, and the Source terminal is common to both input and output loops (typically grounded through a bypass capacitor at AC). The **Frequency Response Characteristic** of this amplifier describes the variation of its voltage gain magnitude $\vert A_v \vert$ and phase $\angle A_v$ as a function of the input signal frequency $f$, typically represented by a **Bode magnitude plot** and a **Bode phase plot**.

The frequency response of any practical amplifier is divided into three distinct regions:

1. **Mid-band region** — Where the gain is constant and maximum.
2. **Low-frequency region** — Where the gain rolls off due to coupling and bypass capacitors.
3. **High-frequency region** — Where the gain rolls off due to internal parasitic capacitances of the MOSFET.

The bandwidth of the amplifier is defined as:
$$B = f_{H} - f_{L} \approx f_{H} \quad (\text{since } f_{H} \gg f_{L})$$

where $f_{L}$ is the **lower cutoff frequency (–3 dB frequency)** and $f_{H}$ is the **upper cutoff frequency (–3 dB frequency)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Scheme lab experiments, students are required to obtain the frequency response of the CS amplifier, plot the gain (in dB) versus frequency on a semi-log graph, and identify the lower cutoff, upper cutoff, and bandwidth.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **car radio** playing music:

- The **mid-band region** is like the normal volume level when you tune to a clear FM station. The sound is loud, undistorted, and the gain is maximum.
- The **low-frequency region** is like the **bass** that fades out when you turn the volume knob too low — the **bass-boost capacitors** in the audio chain can no longer pass the low notes. Similarly, large coupling capacitors stop passing very low frequencies.
- The **high-frequency region** is like the **treble** that fades when the antenna wire is too long — the **internal junction capacitances** of the MOSFET act like tiny storage buckets that cannot charge and discharge fast enough at high frequencies, causing the treble (high frequencies) to be lost.

So the CS amplifier behaves like a **band-pass filter** for voltage signals: it amplifies only a specific band of frequencies (the **mid-band**) and attenuates frequencies outside this band. The **Common Source** configuration is the MOSFET equivalent of the Common Emitter (CE) amplifier for BJTs — it provides both **voltage gain** and **current gain**, in addition to a **180° phase inversion** between input and output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Mid-band voltage gain:** typically **10 to 100** (20 dB to 40 dB).
> - **Phase shift at mid-band:** **180°** (output is inverted).
> - **Cutoff frequencies** are defined at the **–3 dB points** (where gain drops to 0.707 of mid-band value).
> - **Slope of roll-off:** **–20 dB/decade** (or **–6 dB/octave**) per pole outside the band.

---

## 1.3 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of CS Amplifier with Frequency-Dependent Elements

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeI[Vin - AC Input Signal] --> nodeA[Coupling Capacitor Cin]
    nodeA --> nodeB[Gate of MOSFET - High Input Impedance]
    nodeB --> nodeC[MOSFET Transistor - Voltage Controlled Current Source gmVgs]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Drain with RD and Internal Capacitances Cgs Cgd]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Coupling Capacitor Cout]
    nodeE --> nodeO[Vout to Load RL]
    nodeS[Source Terminal with RS and Bypass Capacitor CS] --> nodeB
    nodeS --> nodeO
    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000000
    classDef active fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000000
    class nodeI,nodeA,nodeE,nodeO input
    class nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeS active
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of Common Source MOSFET Amplifier

In a CS amplifier, the MOSFET is biased in the **saturation region** using a voltage-divider or self-bias network. The AC input signal $v_{gs}$ superimposed on the DC bias $V_{GS}$ produces a varying drain current $i_d = g_m \cdot v_{gs}$, where $g_m$ is the **transconductance** of the MOSFET.

**Step-by-step operating logic:**

- **Step 1 — DC Biasing:** The gate is biased at a suitable $V_{GS}$ to establish the Q-point (quiescent point) in the saturation region. $I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$.
- **Step 2 — AC Signal Injection:** The small AC signal enters through the coupling capacitor $C_{in}$, which blocks DC and passes AC to the gate.
- **Step 3 — Voltage-to-Current Conversion:** The MOSFET acts as a **Voltage-Controlled Current Source (VCCS)**. The drain current varies linearly with $v_{gs}$ as $i_d = g_m v_{gs}$.
- **Step 4 — Current-to-Voltage Conversion:** The varying drain current flows through the drain resistor $R_D$ (or $R_D \parallel R_L$), producing an amplified output voltage $v_{ds} = -i_d \cdot R_D$.
- **Step 5 — Output Coupling:** The amplified output (which is **inverted** — 180° phase shift) is delivered to the load through $C_{out}$.
- **Step 6 — Source Bypass:** The source resistor $R_S$ provides DC stability but reduces AC gain; the bypass capacitor $C_S$ shorts $R_S$ at AC, restoring the gain.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Frequency Response

The frequency response is governed by **two distinct sets of reactive elements**:

| Reactive Element | Type | Region of Influence | Physical Origin |
|------------------|------|---------------------|-----------------|
| $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, $C_S$ | External (coupling/bypass) | **Low-frequency** roll-off | Designed large capacitors to block DC |
| $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$ | Internal (parasitic) | **High-frequency** roll-off | MOSFET oxide and junction capacitances |

**Why low frequencies are attenuated:** At very low frequencies, the reactance of coupling capacitors $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ becomes large, forming a voltage divider with the input/output resistances. The signal is partially dropped across the capacitor, reducing the gain.

**Why high frequencies are attenuated:** At very high frequencies, the internal capacitances $C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$ provide low-impedance paths that short-circuit the signal. The **Miller effect** multiplies $C_{gd}$ by the gain factor $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$, dramatically lowering the high-frequency cutoff.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The mid-band is the "sweet spot" where coupling capacitors act as short circuits (negligible impedance) and internal capacitances act as open circuits (negligible admittance). The amplifier behaves as a pure resistive network here, giving a flat, maximum gain.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{th}} = \sqrt{2 k_n \dfrac{W}{L} I_D}$ | Siemens (S) or A/V |
| 2 | Mid-band voltage gain (with bypassed $R_S$) | $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel R_L)$ | Dimensionless, negative sign ⇒ 180° phase inversion |
| 3 | Mid-band voltage gain (unbypassed $R_S$) | $A_v = -\dfrac{g_m (R_D \parallel R_L)}{1 + g_m R_S}$ | Reduced gain, improved stability |
| 4 | Voltage gain in dB | $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ | dB |
| 5 | Lower cutoff frequency (general) | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_{eq} C}$ | Hz, where $R_{eq}$ is the Thevenin resistance seen by the capacitor |
| 6 | Upper cutoff frequency | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_{sig}^{\prime} C_{in}^{\prime}}$ | Hz, where $R_{sig}^{\prime}$ and $C_{in}^{\prime}$ are the effective input resistance and capacitance |
| 7 | Miller (input) capacitance | $C_M = C_{gd} (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | F, due to Miller multiplication |
| 8 | Total input capacitance | $C_{in}^{\prime} = C_{gs} + C_M = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | F |
| 9 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ | Hz |
| 10 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $GBP = \vert A_v \vert \cdot BW$ | Hz (figure of merit) |
| 11 | Pole frequency for a single RC | $f_p = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \tau} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ | Hz |
| 12 | Output voltage (general) | $v_{out} = A_v \cdot v_{in}$ | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder on Markdown Tables:** All vertical bars inside math expressions in the table above are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve the markdown table structure.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio pre-amplifiers** — CS MOSFET amplifiers are widely used in audio equipment for their high input impedance, low noise, and wide bandwidth.
- **RF front-ends** — CS configurations form the first stage of radio receivers due to their high-frequency capability.
- **Operational transconductance amplifiers (OTAs)** — The CS stage is the fundamental building block.
- **Sensor signal conditioning** — High input impedance makes CS amplifiers ideal for interfacing with high-impedance sensors (piezoelectric, biomedical).
- **Communication systems** — Used in intermediate-frequency (IF) stages of superheterodyne receivers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Mid-Band Voltage Gain $A_v$

**Starting Point — Small-Signal Equivalent Circuit (mid-band, all capacitors ideal):**

At mid-band frequencies:
- Coupling capacitors $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$ → **short circuits** (negligible reactance).
- Bypass capacitor $C_S$ → **short circuit** (negligible reactance).
- Internal capacitances $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$ → **open circuits** (negligible admittance).
- The MOSFET is replaced by its small-signal model: a VCCS $g_m v_{gs}$ between drain and source, with output resistance $r_{ds}$ (often neglected for simplicity).

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Let $v_{gs}$ be the small-signal gate-to-source voltage. The small-signal drain current is:
$$i_d = g_m \cdot v_{gs}$$

This drain current flows through the parallel combination of $R_D$ and $R_L$ (since $C_{out}$ is a short at mid-band, $R_L$ appears in parallel with $R_D$):
$$i_d = \frac{-v_{out}}{R_D \parallel R_L}$$

The negative sign appears because a positive $v_{gs}$ causes a positive $i_d$ that produces a voltage drop across $R_D$, making the drain voltage decrease (inversion).

**Equating both expressions for $i_d$:**
$$g_m v_{gs} = \frac{-v_{out}}{R_D \parallel R_L}$$

**Solving for $v_{out} \slash v_{gs}$:**
$$v_{out} = -g_m (R_D \parallel R_L) \cdot v_{gs}$$

Since $v_{gs} = v_{in}$ (the input is applied directly gate-to-source, and gate current is zero):
$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (R_D \parallel R_L)$$

**Final compact result:**
$$\boxed{A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel R_L)}$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase shift** between input and output. The magnitude is:
$$\vert A_v \vert = g_m (R_D \parallel R_L)$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Lower Cutoff Frequency $f_L$ (Dominant Pole Approximation)

At low frequencies, the coupling and bypass capacitors can no longer be treated as short circuits. Each capacitor contributes one **pole** (or **cutoff frequency**). The dominant pole is the one with the **highest** lower cutoff frequency — this dominates the –3 dB point.

**For the input coupling capacitor $C_{in}$:**

The Thevenin resistance seen by $C_{in}$ is:
$$R_{in,eq} = R_{sig} \parallel R_G$$

where $R_{sig}$ is the source resistance and $R_G = R_1 \parallel R_2$ is the gate bias network resistance.

The pole frequency due to $C_{in}$ is:
$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2 \pi (R_{sig} \parallel R_G) C_{in}}$$

**For the output coupling capacitor $C_{out}$:**

The Thevenin resistance seen by $C_{out}$ is:
$$R_{out,eq} = (R_D \parallel R_L) + r_{ds} \approx R_D \parallel R_L$$

The pole frequency is:
$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2 \pi (R_D \parallel R_L) C_{out}}$$

**For the source bypass capacitor $C_S$:**

When $C_S$ is not a perfect short, the gain expression includes the source degeneration factor. The pole frequency is:
$$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2 \pi (R_S \parallel \frac{1}{g_m}) C_S} = \frac{g_m}{2 \pi (1 + g_m R_S) C_S}$$

**Combined lower cutoff frequency (using dominant pole approximation):**

If one pole is much higher than the others:
$$f_L \approx f_{L,\text{dominant}} = \max(f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{L3})$$

**Exact formula (sum of squares, valid for non-interacting poles):**
$$f_L \approx \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{L3}^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In most practical KTU lab circuits, the dominant low-frequency pole is due to $C_S$ (source bypass capacitor), because $R_S \parallel (1/g_m)$ is typically small and $C_S$ has a relatively small capacitance value.

## 3.3 Derivation of Upper Cutoff Frequency $f_H$ (Miller Effect)

**Step 1 — Identify the internal capacitances of the MOSFET:**

- $C_{gs}$ — Gate-to-Source capacitance (oxide capacitance + overlap).
- $C_{gd}$ — Gate-to-Drain capacitance (oxide capacitance + overlap).
- $C_{ds}$ — Drain-to-Source capacitance (junction capacitance, often negligible in CS analysis).

**Step 2 — Apply the Miller Theorem to $C_{gd}$:**

The Miller theorem states that an impedance $Z$ connected between input and output nodes of an inverting amplifier with gain $K = v_{out}/v_{in}$ can be replaced by two impedances:
- Input side: $Z_1 = Z / (1 - K)$
- Output side: $Z_2 = Z \cdot (1 - K) / K$

For $C_{gd}$ with $K = A_v = -g_m(R_D \parallel R_L)$:
$$Z_{gd} = \frac{1}{j \omega C_{gd}}$$

The equivalent input capacitance (Miller capacitance) is:
$$C_M = C_{gd} (1 - K) = C_{gd} (1 + \vert A_v \vert) = C_{gd} (1 + g_m (R_D \parallel R_L))$$

**Step 3 — Total input capacitance:**
$$C_{in}^{\prime} = C_{gs} + C_M = C_{gs} + C_{gd} (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$

**Step 4 — Effective input resistance:**
$$R_{sig}^{\prime} = R_{sig} \parallel R_G$$

**Step 5 — Upper cutoff frequency:**
$$\boxed{f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{sig}^{\prime} C_{in}^{\prime}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi (R_{sig} \parallel R_G) [C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)]}}$$

**Numerical demonstration (typical KTU lab values):**

Let $R_{sig} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_G = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 5 \text{ pF}$, $C_{gd} = 1 \text{ pF}$, $g_m = 5 \text{ mA/V}$, $R_D \parallel R_L = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step A — Mid-band gain:**
$$\vert A_v \vert = g_m (R_D \parallel R_L) = (5 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^3) = 25$$

$$\vert A_v \vert_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(25) = 27.96 \text{ dB} \approx 28 \text{ dB}$$

**Step B — Miller capacitance:**
$$C_M = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert) = (1 \text{ pF})(1 + 25) = 26 \text{ pF}$$

**Step C — Total input capacitance:**
$$C_{in}^{\prime} = C_{gs} + C_M = 5 \text{ pF} + 26 \text{ pF} = 31 \text{ pF}$$

**Step D — Effective input resistance:**
$$R_{sig}^{\prime} = R_{sig} \parallel R_G = 1 \text{ k}\Omega \parallel 100 \text{ k}\Omega = \frac{1 \times 100}{1 + 100} \text{ k}\Omega = 0.990 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 990 \Omega$$

**Step E — Upper cutoff frequency:**
$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi (990)(31 \times 10^{-12})} = \frac{1}{1.928 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 5.19 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step F — Bandwidth:**
$$BW = f_H - f_L \approx 5.19 \text{ MHz} \quad (\text{assuming } f_L \approx 50 \text{ Hz} \ll f_H)$$

**Step G — Gain-Bandwidth Product:**
$$GBP = \vert A_v \vert \times BW = 25 \times 5.19 \times 10^6 = 129.75 \text{ MHz}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The Miller effect increased the effective input capacitance from 6 pF (without Miller) to 31 pF — a **5× increase**. This dramatically reduces the upper cutoff frequency. The dominant high-frequency limitation in CS amplifiers is the **Miller multiplication of $C_{gd}$**.

## 3.4 Bode Plot — Asymptotic Frequency Response

The complete gain expression including all three regions is:

$$A_v(s) = \frac{A_{v,mid}}{\left(1 + \dfrac{s}{\omega_L}\right)\left(1 + \dfrac{\omega_H}{s}\right)} = \frac{A_{v,mid} \cdot s / \omega_L}{\left(1 + \dfrac{s}{\omega_L}\right)\left(1 + \dfrac{s}{\omega_H}\right)}$$

In the **low-frequency asymptote** (Bode plot):
- For $f \ll f_L$: gain rises at **+20 dB/decade**.
- At $f = f_L$: gain is **–3 dB** below mid-band value.
- For $f_L < f < f_H$: gain is **constant** (mid-band plateau).
- For $f > f_H$: gain falls at **–20 dB/decade**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Common Source MOSFET Amplifier — Circuit Schematic (Mermaid Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BIAS["DC Biasing Network"]
        nodeVDD[VDD - Positive Supply]
        nodeR1[R1 - Upper Bias Resistor]
        nodeR2[R2 - Lower Bias Resistor]
    end
    subgraph SIGNAL["AC Signal Path"]
        nodeVSig[Vin - AC Signal Source]
        nodeRSig[Rsig - Source Resistance]
        nodeCin[Coupling Capacitor Cin]
        nodeGate[Gate of M1]
    end
    subgraph AMP["MOSFET Amplification Stage"]
        nodeM1[MOSFET M1 - Common Source]
        nodeRD[RD - Drain Resistor]
        nodeRS[RS - Source Resistor]
        nodeCS[Bypass Capacitor CS]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["AC Output Path"]
        nodeCout[Coupling Capacitor Cout]
        nodeRL[Load Resistor RL]
        nodeVout[Vout - AC Output]
    end
    nodeVSig --> nodeRSig --> nodeCin --> nodeGate
    nodeVDD --> nodeR1 --> nodeGate
    nodeGate --> nodeR2 --> nodeGND1[Ground Reference]
    nodeGate --> nodeM1
    nodeVDD --> nodeRD --> nodeM1
    nodeM1 --> nodeRS --> nodeCS --> nodeGND2[Ground Reference]
    nodeM1 --> nodeCout --> nodeRL --> nodeVout
    nodeRL --> nodeGND3[Ground Reference]
    classDef bias fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000000
    classDef sig fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000000
    classDef amp fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000000
    classDef out fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000000
    classDef gnd fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F,color:#000000
    class nodeVDD,nodeR1,nodeR2 bias
    class nodeVSig,nodeRSig,nodeCin,nodeGate sig
    class nodeM1,nodeRD,nodeRS,nodeCS amp
    class nodeCout,nodeRL,nodeVout out
    class nodeGND1,nodeGND2,nodeGND3 gnd
```

## 4.2 Frequency Response — Bode Magnitude Plot (Block Flow of Asymptotic Regions)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Region 1 - Very Low Frequency - f less than fL] -->|"+20 dB/decade slope"| nodeB[Pole at fL - 3 dB Point]
    nodeB -->|"Flat plateau - Constant gain Amid"| nodeC[Region 2 - Mid-Band - fL to fH]
    nodeC -->|"Cutoff at fH - 3 dB Point"| nodeD[Region 3 - High Frequency - f greater than fH]
    nodeD -->|"-20 dB/decade slope"| nodeE[Attenuation Continues]
    classDef low fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000000
    classDef mid fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000000
    classDef high fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000000
    class nodeA,nodeB low
    class nodeC mid
    class nodeD,nodeE high
```

## 4.3 Small-Signal Equivalent Circuit — High-Frequency Model (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn[Input Node - vin] --> nodeR1[Rsig prime]
    nodeR1 --> nodeG[Gate Node]
    nodeG --> nodeCgs[Cgs - Gate to Source]
    nodeCgs --> nodeS[Source Node - AC Ground]
    nodeG --> nodeCgd[Cgd - Gate to Drain - Miller Capacitance]
    nodeCgd --> nodeD[Drain Node]
    nodeS --> nodeVCCS[Voltage Controlled Current Source - gm vgs]
    nodeVCCS --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeRDprime[RD parallel RL]
    nodeRDprime --> nodeS
    nodeD --> nodeVout[vout]
    classDef sig fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000000
    classDef cap fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000000
    classDef active fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000000
    classDef res fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000000
    class nodeIn,nodeG,nodeD,nodeS,nodeVout sig
    class nodeCgs,nodeCgd cap
    class nodeVCCS active
    class nodeR1,nodeRDprime res
```

## 4.4 Frequency Response Curve — Graphical Description

The Bode magnitude plot of the CS amplifier has the following key landmarks:

| Frequency | Gain (dB) | Behavior |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| $f \to 0$ | $-\infty$ | Complete attenuation by coupling/bypass capacitors |
| $f = f_L$ | $A_{v,mid}(\text{dB}) - 3$ | Lower –3 dB cutoff |
| $f_L < f < f_H$ | $A_{v,mid}(\text{dB})$ | Flat mid-band plateau |
| $f = f_H$ | $A_{v,mid}(\text{dB}) - 3$ | Upper –3 dB cutoff |
| $f \to \infty$ | $-\infty$ | Attenuation by internal capacitances |

**Phase response:**
- At $f \ll f_L$: phase $\approx +90°$ (one zero contribution from coupling).
- At $f = f_L$: phase $\approx +135°$.
- At mid-band: phase $\approx 180°$ (inversion).
- At $f = f_H$: phase $\approx 225°$.
- At $f \gg f_H$: phase $\approx 270°$ (additional 90° lag from Miller pole).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
**Define the term "frequency response" of a MOSFET amplifier. What is meant by the –3 dB cutoff frequency?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **frequency response** of a MOSFET amplifier is a plot of the magnitude of the voltage gain $\vert A_v \vert$ (usually in dB) versus the input signal frequency $f$ (usually on a logarithmic scale). It describes how the amplifier's gain varies with frequency and identifies the range of frequencies over which the amplifier provides useful amplification.

The **–3 dB cutoff frequency** is the frequency at which the voltage gain of the amplifier drops to **0.707 times** (or $1/\sqrt{2}$ times) its mid-band maximum value. In dB, this corresponds to a drop of **3 dB** below the mid-band gain level. There are two such frequencies:
- $f_L$ — **Lower cutoff frequency** (lower edge of the pass-band).
- $f_H$ — **Upper cutoff frequency** (upper edge of the pass-band).

The **bandwidth** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ defines the useful operating range of the amplifier. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**What is the Miller effect? How does it affect the high-frequency response of a Common Source MOSFET amplifier?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Miller effect** (or **Miller theorem**) states that an impedance $Z$ connected between the input and output of an inverting amplifier with voltage gain $K$ appears at the input as an impedance $Z \slash (1 - K)$ and at the output as $Z (1 - K) \slash K$.

In a **Common Source MOSFET amplifier**, the internal gate-to-drain capacitance $C_{gd}$ is subjected to this Miller multiplication. The effective input capacitance becomes:
$$C_{in}^{\prime} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$

The Miller-multiplied capacitance $C_M = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ is typically much larger than $C_{gs}$. This **dramatically increases the total input capacitance**, which in turn **lowers the upper cutoff frequency** $f_H$ according to:
$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{sig}^{\prime} C_{in}^{\prime}}$$

Hence, the Miller effect is the **dominant factor** that limits the high-frequency response of CS amplifiers. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module 2 Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a small-signal equivalent circuit, derive the expression for the **mid-band voltage gain** of a Common Source MOSFET amplifier. State clearly any assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CS MOSFET amplifier has the following parameters: $g_m = 4 \text{ mA/V}$, $R_D = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 4 \text{ pF}$, $C_{gd} = 1 \text{ pF}$, $R_{sig} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_G = 200 \text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate:
- (i) The mid-band voltage gain in dB.
- (ii) The Miller capacitance and total input capacitance.
- (iii) The upper cutoff frequency $f_H$.
- (iv) The bandwidth of the amplifier. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Mid-Band Voltage Gain:**

In the mid-band region:
- All coupling and bypass capacitors act as **short circuits**.
- All internal capacitances act as **open circuits**.
- The MOSFET is replaced by its small-signal model: a **VCCS $g_m v_{gs}$** between drain and source.

The small-signal drain current is:
$$i_d = g_m v_{gs}$$

This current flows through the effective drain load $R_{D}^{\prime} = R_D \parallel R_L$. The output voltage is:
$$v_{out} = -i_d \cdot (R_D \parallel R_L) = -g_m v_{gs} (R_D \parallel R_L)$$

Since the gate draws no current, $v_{gs} = v_{in}$:
$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (R_D \parallel R_L)$$

**Assumptions:**
- MOSFET is in saturation region.
- Output resistance $r_{ds} \to \infty$ (channel-length modulation neglected).
- All capacitors are ideal.
- Signal is small-signal (linear operation). **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculations:**

**Step (i) — Mid-band voltage gain:**
$$R_D \parallel R_L = \frac{5 \times 10}{5 + 10} = \frac{50}{15} = 3.333 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$\vert A_v \vert = g_m (R_D \parallel R_L) = (4 \times 10^{-3})(3.333 \times 10^3) = 13.33$$
$$A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(13.33) = 22.5 \text{ dB}$$

**Step (ii) — Miller and total input capacitance:**
$$C_M = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert) = (1 \text{ pF})(1 + 13.33) = 14.33 \text{ pF}$$
$$C_{in}^{\prime} = C_{gs} + C_M = 4 \text{ pF} + 14.33 \text{ pF} = 18.33 \text{ pF}$$

**Step (iii) — Upper cutoff frequency:**
$$R_{sig}^{\prime} = R_{sig} \parallel R_G = \frac{1 \times 200}{1 + 200} = 0.995 \text{ k}\Omega = 995 \Omega$$
$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi (995)(18.33 \times 10^{-12})} = \frac{1}{1.146 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 8.73 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step (iv) — Bandwidth:**
Assuming $f_L \approx 50 \text{ Hz}$ (typical lab value):
$$BW = f_H - f_L \approx 8.73 \text{ MHz} - 50 \text{ Hz} \approx 8.73 \text{ MHz}$$

$$\boxed{\vert A_v \vert_{\text{dB}} = 22.5 \text{ dB}, \quad C_M = 14.33 \text{ pF}, \quad C_{in}^{\prime} = 18.33 \text{ pF}, \quad f_H \approx 8.73 \text{ MHz}, \quad BW \approx 8.73 \text{ MHz}}$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 2 Expected Pattern — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **low-frequency response** of a CS MOSFET amplifier. Derive the expressions for the lower cutoff frequencies contributed by the coupling capacitor $C_{in}$, the output coupling capacitor $C_{out}$, and the source bypass capacitor $C_S$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **Bode plot**, describe the **complete frequency response** of a CS amplifier. Explain the meaning of the three regions — low-frequency, mid-band, and high-frequency. Discuss how the bandwidth is measured experimentally in the KTU lab. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Low-Frequency Response:**

At low frequencies, the reactance of the coupling and bypass capacitors $X_C = 1 \slash (2\pi f C)$ becomes significant, and they can no longer be treated as short circuits. The amplifier's gain begins to fall.

**Pole due to $C_{in}$:**

The Thevenin resistance seen by $C_{in}$ is $R_{in,eq} = R_{sig} \parallel R_G$, where $R_{sig}$ is the source resistance and $R_G = R_1 \parallel R_2$ is the gate bias network resistance.
$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2 \pi (R_{sig} \parallel R_G) C_{in}}$$

**Pole due to $C_{out}$:**

The Thevenin resistance seen by $C_{out}$ is the series combination of $R_D$ (looking back into the drain) and $R_L$ (the load):
$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2 \pi (R_D + R_L) C_{out}}$$

**Pole due to $C_S$:**

The effective resistance seen by $C_S$ is $R_S \parallel (1 \slash g_m)$:
$$f_{L3} = \frac{g_m}{2 \pi (1 + g_m R_S) C_S} \approx \frac{1}{2 \pi (R_S \parallel \frac{1}{g_m}) C_S}$$

**Overall lower cutoff frequency (dominant pole approximation):**
$$f_L \approx \max(f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{L3})$$

In most practical CS amplifiers, $f_{L3}$ is the dominant pole because $R_S \parallel (1 \slash g_m)$ is small and the bypass capacitor is relatively small. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — Bode Plot and Experimental Bandwidth Measurement:**

**Bode Magnitude Plot Description:**

The Bode magnitude plot has three distinct regions:

1. **Low-Frequency Region ($f < f_L$):** Gain rises with frequency at a slope of **+20 dB/decade** (or +6 dB/octave). This is because the coupling/bypass capacitors progressively offer lower reactance, allowing more of the signal to pass. The gain is:
$$A_v(f) \approx A_{v,mid} \cdot \frac{f}{f_L} \quad \text{for } f \ll f_L$$

2. **Mid-Band Region ($f_L < f < f_H$):** Gain is **constant** at the maximum value $A_{v,mid}$. All coupling/bypass capacitors are effective shorts; all internal capacitances are effective opens. The amplifier behaves as a **purely resistive** network.

3. **High-Frequency Region ($f > f_H$):** Gain falls with frequency at a slope of **–20 dB/decade** (or –6 dB/octave) due to the shunting effect of $C_{gs}$ and the Miller-multiplied $C_{gd}$.

**Experimental Bandwidth Measurement in KTU Lab:**

1. Apply a small AC sine wave (typically 10–50 mV peak, 1 kHz) at the input of the CS amplifier using a function generator.
2. Measure the mid-band output voltage $V_{out,mid}$ and compute the mid-band gain $A_{v,mid} = V_{out,mid} \slash V_{in}$.
3. Convert to dB: $A_{v,mid}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(A_{v,mid})$.
4. **For lower cutoff $f_L$:** Decrease the input frequency from the mid-band value until the output drops to **0.707 times** the mid-band value. The corresponding frequency is $f_L$.
5. **For upper cutoff $f_H$:** Increase the input frequency until the output again drops to 0.707 times the mid-band value. The corresponding frequency is $f_H$.
6. **Bandwidth:** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$.
7. Plot the gain (in dB) on the **y-axis** versus frequency (on a **logarithmic x-axis**) on a semi-log graph sheet to obtain the Bode plot.

**(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $A_v = -g_m(R_D \parallel R_L)$ — students often write only the magnitude, losing 1 mark for missing the 180° phase inversion.
> 2. **Ignoring $R_L$** — many students use $R_D$ alone instead of $R_D \parallel R_L$ when the load is connected. Always check whether the problem specifies a load resistor.
> 3. **Confusing $R_S$ bypassed vs. unbypassed** — if $C_S$ is present, the mid-band gain uses $R_D \parallel R_L$ only; if absent, the gain is divided by $(1 + g_m R_S)$.
> 4. **Miller effect miscalculation** — students often write $C_M = C_{gd} \cdot A_v$ instead of $C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. The "+1" is crucial.
> 5. **Units inconsistency** — frequencies must be in **Hz**, capacitances in **F** (not pF) when applying the formula. Always convert before calculation.
> 6. **Mid-band assumption violation** — applying mid-band formulas at low or high frequencies is a common conceptual error. Always identify the region first.
> 7. **Confusing bandwidth with gain-bandwidth product** — bandwidth is in Hz, GBP is the product $\vert A_v \vert \cdot BW$ and represents the trade-off between gain and bandwidth.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points — Must Remember Before Exam:**

- **CS Amplifier:** Input at Gate, Output at Drain, Source is common (grounded at AC).
- **Mid-band gain formula:** $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel R_L)$ — **negative sign** = **180° inversion**.
- **Gain in dB:** $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$.
- **Transconductance:** $g_m = 2 I_D \slash (V_{GS} - V_{th})$ — depends on Q-point.
- **Low-frequency roll-off:** caused by $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, $C_S$ — slope **+20 dB/decade** as frequency increases into mid-band.
- **High-frequency roll-off:** caused by $C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$ — slope **–20 dB/decade** as frequency increases beyond mid-band.
- **Miller effect:** $C_{gd}$ is multiplied by $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ at the input — this is the **dominant** high-frequency limitation.
- **Cutoff frequencies:** defined at **–3 dB** points (gain = 0.707 of mid-band value).
- **Bandwidth:** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$.
- **Phase at mid-band:** **180°** (output inverted relative to input).
- **Experimental procedure:** vary input frequency, measure output, find –3 dB points, plot semi-log Bode plot.
- **Source bypass capacitor $C_S$:** bypasses $R_S$ at mid-band to restore gain while keeping DC stability.
- **Dominant low-frequency pole:** usually due to $C_S$ in practical circuits.
- **Dominant high-frequency pole:** due to Miller-multiplied $C_{gd}$.
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product** is a **constant** for a given amplifier — increasing gain reduces bandwidth and vice versa.
- **Typical Bode plot shape:** low-frequency rise (+20 dB/decade) → mid-band plateau → high-frequency fall (–20 dB/decade) — this is a **band-pass** characteristic.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Op-Amp Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Op-Amp Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 Module Experiment 2: Measurement of Op-Amp Parameters

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a high-gain, DC-coupled electronic voltage amplifier with differential inputs and, usually, a single-ended output. In real-world fabrication, an op-amp is built from dozens of transistors, resistors, and a single capacitor on a silicon die. Because it is a *physical* device and not an *ideal* mathematical construct, its behaviour deviates from the textbook ideal in measurable, quantifiable ways.

**Op-Amp Parameters** are the finite, non-ideal electrical quantities that describe how closely a real op-amp approaches the "ideal op-amp" model assumed in introductory circuit analysis. Measuring these parameters is a fundamental laboratory exercise because:

1. They dictate the **DC accuracy** of any precision circuit (instrumentation amplifier, ADC front-end, integrator).
2. They determine the **AC performance** (bandwidth, distortion, settling time).
3. They reveal the **practical limits** of circuits that the ideal model hides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Verbatim Syllabus Spirit):**
> *The experiment aims to experimentally determine the static and dynamic non-ideal parameters of a general-purpose op-amp such as the **µA741 / LM741** (or equivalent), including input offset voltage, input bias current, input offset current, open-loop voltage gain, common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR), slew rate, and unity-gain bandwidth.*

### 1.2 Parameters Classified (High-Level Map)

| Class | Parameter | Symbol | What it tells us |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DC / Static** | Input Offset Voltage | $V_{IO}$ | DC error at the input that produces 0 V at the output |
| **DC / Static** | Input Bias Current | $I_{B}$ | Average DC current drawn by the two input pins |
| **DC / Static** | Input Offset Current | $I_{IO}$ | Mismatch between the two bias currents |
| **AC / Small-Signal** | Open-Loop Voltage Gain | $A_{OL}$ | Gain of the bare op-amp with no feedback |
| **AC / Small-Signal** | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | CMRR | Ability to reject signals common to both inputs |
| **AC / Large-Signal** | Slew Rate | $SR$ | Maximum rate of change of output voltage |
| **AC / Frequency** | Unity-Gain Bandwidth / Gain-Bandwidth Product | $GBW$ or $f_T$ | Frequency at which open-loop gain drops to 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> The **ideal op-amp** assumes: infinite open-loop gain, infinite input impedance, zero input bias current, zero input offset voltage, zero output impedance, infinite CMRR, infinite bandwidth, and infinite slew rate. Every real op-amp parameter is a *quantified deviation* from this ideal.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **perfect bathroom weighing scale** (the *ideal op-amp*). It would:
- Be exactly accurate at zero (zero offset).
- Not bend at all when you stand on it (infinite stiffness → infinite gain).
- Weigh a 70 kg person the same way regardless of where on the platform they stand (infinite CMRR).
- Respond instantly when you step on (infinite bandwidth, infinite slew rate).

A **real bathroom scale** has a tiny offset (it reads 0.2 kg when empty), bends a little when you stand on it (finite gain), gives slightly different readings depending on where you stand (finite CMRR), and takes a second to settle on the final number (finite bandwidth / slew rate).

**Op-amp parameters are the engineering "spec sheet" of the imperfections of the real scale.** The lab exercise is essentially *putting the op-amp on a test bench and weighing its imperfections*.

> [!TIP]
> **Why measure instead of just read the datasheet?**
> Datasheet values are *typical* and are measured under strictly controlled conditions (specific supply voltage, temperature, frequency). In a lab, we are using a *specific physical IC on a specific breadboard at room temperature* with our own instruments. The measured value will closely match the datasheet, and the small deviation is itself a lesson about component tolerances, breadboard parasitic capacitance, instrument loading, and thermal effects.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Parameters — Theory of Operation

### 2.1.1 Input Offset Voltage ($V_{IO}$)

**Conceptual meaning:** Because the internal transistor pairs of the input differential stage are not perfectly matched during fabrication, the output is not exactly 0 V when *both* inputs are grounded. The tiny equivalent differential input voltage that, if applied, would drive the output back to 0 V is called the **input offset voltage**.

**Why it exists:** Two reasons —
1. Mismatch in the base-emitter voltages ($V_{BE}$) of the input differential pair transistors.
2. Mismatch in the collector-load resistors of the input stage.

**Mathematical model:** It is modelled as a small DC voltage source $V_{IO}$ in series with one of the input terminals.

**Standard measurement circuit:** Op-amp is wired in a unity-gain (voltage follower) configuration with the non-inverting input grounded. The DC voltage measured at the output equals the input offset voltage (since the closed-loop gain is 1, the offset is passed through 1:1).

**Mitigation:** Use offset-null pins (on 741) with a potentiometer, or use an op-amp with chopper-stabilized input (e.g., ICL7650, LTC1050) for ultra-low-offset applications.

---

### 2.1.2 Input Bias Current ($I_B$)

**Conceptual meaning:** Because the input stage of a BJT op-amp is a pair of NPN/PNP transistors, their bases must draw a small DC current to keep them biased in the active region. This is the **input bias current**, $I_B$. For a JFET-input op-amp (e.g., TL08x, LF35x), this current is the leakage current of the gate junctions — typically in picoamperes.

**Why it exists:** BJT bases need base current to operate; $I_B = (I_{B+} + I_{B-})/2$, where $I_{B+}$ is the current into the non-inverting input and $I_{B-}$ is the current into the inverting input.

**Standard measurement method:** Apply a known non-inverting input resistor $R_1$ (typically 1 MΩ), measure the output voltage, and use the fact that the input bias current flows through this resistor producing a DC error voltage.

**Mitigation:** Use op-amps with FET inputs (LF351, TL081) when very high source impedance is involved.

---

### 2.1.3 Input Offset Current ($I_{IO}$)

**Conceptual meaning:** The *difference* between the two bias currents. $I_{IO} = \vert I_{B+} - I_{B-} \vert$.

**Why it matters:** Even if both bias currents are large, if they are *equal* they produce the same voltage drop across matched source resistors and cancel out at the differential input. The *offset* is what produces the residual error.

**Mathematical relationship:** For a non-inverting amplifier with input resistor $R_1$ and feedback resistor $R_f$, the output DC error due to $I_{IO}$ is $V_{O,err} = I_{IO} \times R_f$.

---

### 2.1.4 Open-Loop Voltage Gain ($A_{OL}$)

**Conceptual meaning:** The differential gain of the op-amp with *no feedback applied*. For the 741, this is on the order of $2 \times 10^5$ (106 dB). It is heavily frequency-dependent.

**Why it exists:** The gain is set by the transconductance of the input stage and the impedance of the active load. It is intentionally made very high so that negative feedback can reduce gain to a precise, controlled value at the cost of bandwidth.

**Measurement challenge:** You *cannot* directly measure the open-loop gain with a multimeter in DC mode because any tiny noise or offset will saturate the output. The standard technique is to use a **closed-loop test circuit with a very low-frequency AC signal** and infer the open-loop gain from the closed-loop behaviour.

---

### 2.1.5 Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)

**Conceptual meaning:** The ratio of differential-mode gain to common-mode gain, expressed in dB. It quantifies how well the op-amp rejects signals that appear *identically* on both inputs (e.g., 50 Hz mains hum picked up on a long cable).

**Why it exists:** Even with perfect transistor matching, the finite output impedance of the tail current source and Early effect mismatch cause some common-mode signal to "leak" to the output.

**Measurement:** Apply the same AC signal to *both* inputs (i.e., drive the inputs in parallel) and measure the output. The ratio of input signal to output signal (× closed-loop gain) gives the CMRR.

---

### 2.1.6 Slew Rate ($SR$)

**Conceptual meaning:** The maximum rate at which the output voltage can change, measured in V/µs. It is set by the maximum current the input stage can deliver to the internal compensation capacitor.

**Why it exists:** The 741 has an internal 30 pF Miller compensation capacitor. The input stage can deliver a maximum of ~20 µA, giving $SR = I_{max}/C = 20\,\mu A / 30\,pF \approx 0.67\,V/\mu s$ (the famous "0.5 V/µs" datasheet figure).

**Measurement:** Apply a large-amplitude square wave (e.g., ±10 V peak-to-peak at 1 kHz) to the input of a unity-gain follower and observe the output on an oscilloscope. The output will be a *trapezoid* with linear rising/falling edges. Measure the slope of the linear edge — that slope *is* the slew rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slew rate is a *large-signal* parameter** (the op-amp is being driven hard, into saturation-like current-limit). It is **not** the same as the small-signal bandwidth. A 741 can amplify a 1 kHz sine wave of small amplitude perfectly, but a 1 kHz, 20 V peak-to-peak sine wave will become visibly triangular because the required $\frac{dV}{dt}$ exceeds the slew rate.

---

### 2.1.7 Unity-Gain Bandwidth / Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)

**Conceptual meaning:** The frequency at which the open-loop gain has dropped to 1 (0 dB). For a 741, this is approximately **1 MHz**. For every doubling of closed-loop gain, the closed-loop bandwidth halves — the product of the two remains roughly constant, which is why it is called the **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)**.

**Mathematical form:** $\text{GBW} = A_{CL} \times f_{-3dB}$ where $A_{CL}$ is the closed-loop gain and $f_{-3dB}$ is the corresponding closed-loop bandwidth.

**Why it exists:** Internal compensation capacitor creates a dominant pole. The gain rolls off at –20 dB/decade beyond this pole.

**Measurement:** Configure the op-amp as a non-inverting amplifier with gain 10. Sweep the input frequency. The frequency at which the output drops to 70.7% of the mid-band value is the –3 dB bandwidth. GBW ≈ 10 × that frequency.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Typical Value (µA741) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Input Offset Voltage** | $V_{IO} = V_{O}/A_{CL}$ (measured in voltage follower) | $\le 6\,\text{mV}$ | mV |
| 2 | **Input Bias Current** | $I_B = \vert V_{O} \vert / \left[ R_1 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \right]$ | $\le 500\,\text{nA}$ | nA |
| 3 | **Input Offset Current** | $I_{IO} = \vert V_{O2} - V_{O1} \vert / R_f$ | $\le 200\,\text{nA}$ | nA |
| 4 | **Open-Loop Voltage Gain** | $A_{OL} = (R_f / R_1) \times (V_{in} / V_{out,error})$ | $\approx 2 \times 10^5$ | V/V (or dB: 106 dB) |
| 5 | **CMRR** | $\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{A_{DM}}{A_{CM}} \right) = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{V_{in,CM}}{V_{out}/A_{CL}} \right)$ | $\ge 70\,\text{dB}$ | dB |
| 6 | **Slew Rate** | $SR = \Delta V_{out} / \Delta t$ (from scope edge) | $0.5\,\text{V/µs}$ | V/µs |
| 7 | **Unity-Gain Bandwidth (GBW)** | $\text{GBW} = A_{CL} \times f_{3dB,CL}$ | $1\,\text{MHz}$ | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention in the lab:** All offset values are *magnitudes* (absolute values). The op-amp can produce either polarity of offset depending on which transistor is stronger. We always report $\vert V_{IO} \vert$, $\vert I_{IO} \vert$, etc.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Parameter | Critical Application |
|---|---|
| $V_{IO}$ | **Strain gauge bridges**, **thermocouple amplifiers** — small signals cannot tolerate millivolt offsets. |
| $I_B$ | **Photodiode transimpedance amplifiers** — photodiode source impedance is huge, $I_B$ through it = large voltage error. |
| CMRR | **ECG / EEG front ends**, **audio balanced line receivers** — 50/60 Hz mains interference must be rejected by >80 dB. |
| $SR$ | **Video amplifiers**, **audio power amps**, **PWM generation** — fast edges demand high slew rate. |
| GBW | **High-speed ADC drivers**, **active filters** at high frequency — closed-loop bandwidth must exceed signal bandwidth. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Input Offset Voltage from Voltage Follower

**Setup:** Op-amp wired as a unity-gain follower ($A_{CL} = 1$). The non-inverting input is grounded. The output is not exactly 0 V — it is some small DC voltage $V_O$.

**Analysis:** In the ideal voltage follower, $V_{out} = V_{in}$. With $V_{in} = 0$, the ideal $V_{out} = 0$. The deviation is caused by the internal offset source $V_{IO}$. Because the closed-loop gain is 1, this source appears at the output multiplied by 1.

$$V_O = V_{IO} \cdot A_{CL} = V_{IO} \cdot 1 = V_{IO}$$

**Therefore:**

$$\boxed{V_{IO} = V_O \quad (\text{measured at the output of a unity-gain follower})}$$

**Worked numerical example:** Output measured = 4.3 mV. Then $V_{IO} = 4.3\,\text{mV}$. This is within the 741's $\le 6\,\text{mV}$ specification. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Input Bias Current from Non-Inverting Amplifier

**Setup:** Non-inverting amplifier with $R_1$ from inverting input to ground, $R_f$ as feedback. Input is grounded ($V_{in} = 0$).

**Why ground $V_{in}$?** To remove the effect of the input signal and isolate the bias current contribution.

**Step-by-step nodal analysis at the inverting node ($V^-$):**

Let $I_{B-}$ be the bias current *into* the inverting input. KCL at the inverting node says the current flowing *from* the inverting node through $R_1$ to ground, plus the current flowing through $R_f$ from output to inverting node, must equal $I_{B-}$:

$$I_{R_1} + I_{R_f} = I_{B-}$$

$$I_{R_1} = \frac{V^- - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V^-}{R_1}, \quad I_{R_f} = \frac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_f}$$

For an ideal op-amp with infinite gain, $V^- = V^+ = 0$ (virtual ground).

Substituting:

$$\frac{0}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - 0}{R_f} = I_{B-}$$

$$V_{out} = I_{B-} \cdot R_f$$

By symmetry, if we ground the inverting input and put the resistor on the non-inverting side, $V_{out} = -I_{B+} \cdot R_f$ (with sign convention).

**Standard lab simplification:** Take the *average* of the magnitude of the two output readings when the input resistor is on either side, or measure with both bias currents flowing into the inverting node and use:

$$V_{out} = I_B \cdot R_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

Wait — let us derive the **practical** lab formula cleanly.

**Practical measurement circuit (KTU lab standard):** A resistor $R$ (typically 1 MΩ) is placed in series with the non-inverting input, and the input is driven through this resistor. To measure $I_B$ alone (independent of offset), the trick is:

Use a non-inverting configuration with $R_1$ and $R_f$. With $V_{in} = 0$, the output is:

$$V_O = (I_{B+}) \cdot R_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \quad \text{(approx.)}$$

To get *just* the bias current contribution, the standard textbook formula (Boylestad, Sedra) is:

$$\boxed{I_B = \frac{\vert V_{O2} - V_{O1} \vert}{R_1 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \cdot R_{ref}}}$$

where $V_{O1}$ is measured with the input shorted, $V_{O2}$ is measured with a reference resistor $R_{ref}$ in series with the non-inverting input.

**Simpler KTU formula (commonly tested):** If a single resistor $R$ is connected in series with the non-inverting input and the input is grounded, the output offset due to $I_B$ is $V_O = I_B \cdot R \cdot (1 + R_f/R_1)$. With $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_f = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R = 1\,\text{M}\Omega$, and measured $V_O = 0.45\,\text{V}$:

$$I_B = \frac{V_O}{R \cdot (1 + R_f/R_1)} = \frac{0.45}{10^6 \cdot 101} = \frac{0.45}{1.01 \times 10^8} \approx 4.46\,\text{nA}$$

This is well within the 741's 500 nA spec. ✅

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Input Offset Current

**Setup:** Measure output with the non-inverting input *grounded directly* ($R_{in} = 0$). Then measure output with a *large* resistor in the non-inverting input path. The *change* in output is due solely to the *mismatch* in the two bias currents.

$$V_{O1} = V_{IO} \cdot A_{CL} \quad \text{(offset voltage effect, } R_{in} = 0\text{)}$$

$$V_{O2} = V_{IO} \cdot A_{CL} + I_{IO} \cdot R_{in} \cdot A_{CL}$$

Therefore:

$$V_{O2} - V_{O1} = I_{IO} \cdot R_{in} \cdot A_{CL}$$

Solving for the offset current:

$$\boxed{I_{IO} = \frac{\vert V_{O2} - V_{O1} \vert}{R_{in} \cdot A_{CL}} = \frac{\vert V_{O2} - V_{O1} \vert}{R_{in} \cdot (1 + R_f/R_1)}}$$

**Worked numerical example:** With $R_{in} = 1\,\text{M}\Omega$, $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_f = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$ (so $A_{CL} = 101$), $V_{O1} = 0.4\,\text{V}$, $V_{O2} = 0.58\,\text{V}$:

$$I_{IO} = \frac{0.58 - 0.4}{10^6 \cdot 101} = \frac{0.18}{1.01 \times 10^8} \approx 1.78\,\text{nA}$$

This is within the 741's 200 nA spec. ✅

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Open-Loop Voltage Gain (AC Method)

**Setup:** A pure DC measurement of $A_{OL}$ is impossible because the op-amp will saturate from any tiny offset. The trick is to use an **AC signal at a low frequency** (e.g., 5–10 Hz) and configure the op-amp so that the feedback loop is DC-stable but AC-injects the signal in such a way that we can measure the open-loop behaviour.

**Standard circuit:** A non-inverting amplifier is used, but the AC signal is injected through a large series resistor at the inverting input, and the feedback is taken through a much smaller resistor. The closed-loop gain is *very close to 1* (almost unity follower) so that a tiny input produces a tiny output that is essentially the open-loop behaviour scaled by the loop gain.

**Simplified formula used in the KTU lab:**
