topics.title: Wien Bridge Oscillator Using Op-Amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Wien Bridge Oscillator Using Op-Amp

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Wien Bridge Oscillator** is a *low-frequency sinusoidal oscillator* (typically 1 Hz to 1 MHz) that uses an **operational amplifier** as the active gain element in conjunction with a *lead-lag RC feedback network* (a series RC arm and a parallel RC arm) to produce a sustained, low-distortion sine wave output. It is derived from the classical *Wheatstone bridge* principle, where the bridge is brought to balance to determine the oscillation frequency.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this experiment is classified under the **Barkhausen Criterion** family of sine-wave oscillators, and is considered the *de-facto standard* for generating audio-range frequencies in laboratory instrumentation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Perpetual Swing

Imagine a child on a swing. The swing naturally oscillates at a **fixed natural frequency** determined by the rope length. To keep it going forever (without anyone pushing), two things are needed:

1. **Correct timing** — the push must be applied at exactly the *natural frequency* (this is the **frequency-selective network**).
2. **Enough energy** — each push must compensate for friction loss, neither too weak (swing dies out) nor too strong (swing becomes violent / saturates).

The **op-amp** is the "person pushing" (providing gain), and the **RC lead-lag network** is the "swing mechanism" that determines *when* and *how much* signal must be fed back. The amplitude-stabilising resistors (R₁ and R_f) act as the "thermostat" that adjusts push strength so the swing stays at a perfect, constant height.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCRAL307 — Module 2):** The experiment tests three core student outcomes: (i) design of the RC frequency-determining network, (ii) selection of the feedback gain to satisfy the **Barkhausen criterion**, and (iii) hardware realisation on a breadboard with measurement of frequency and amplitude using a CRO.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two absolute constants to memorise for KTU viva:**
> - The closed-loop gain of the non-inverting amplifier stage **must equal 3** ($A = 3$).
> - The feedback factor of the Wien network **must equal 1/3** ($\beta = 1/3$).
> - The product $A \cdot \beta = 1$ exactly — this is the **Barkhausen condition** for sustained oscillation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The Wien Bridge Oscillator is constructed from **two cascaded sub-systems**:

### Sub-System A — The Frequency-Selective Lead-Lag Network (β-Network)
This is a passive RC network made of:
- **Series arm:** $R_1$ in series with $C_1$
- **Parallel arm:** $R_2$ in parallel with $C_2$

The output of this network ($V_f$, the feedback voltage) is taken from the junction of the two arms and fed back to the non-inverting input (+) of the op-amp. This network acts as a **band-pass filter** that passes only one frequency with *zero phase shift*; all other frequencies are attenuated and phase-shifted.

### Sub-System B — The Non-Inverting Amplifier (A-Stage)
A standard non-inverting op-amp configuration using:
- **Input resistor** $R_i$ between the inverting input and ground
- **Feedback resistor** $R_f$ between the output and the inverting input

This stage provides voltage gain $A = 1 + R_f / R_i$ with *zero phase shift* between its input and output.

### Sub-System C — Why the Loop is Self-Sustaining
The output of the op-amp is connected back into the β-network. The signal therefore travels in a **closed loop**:

> Output (op-amp) → RC Network → Feedback to (+) input → Amplified again → Output … (repeats forever)

For oscillation, the loop gain must be **exactly unity** ($|A\beta| = 1$) and the **total phase shift around the loop must be zero** (or an integer multiple of $2\pi$). The non-inverting amp contributes $0°$ and the Wien network contributes $0°$ at one specific frequency — so the criterion is satisfied naturally at *one* frequency.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Standard Units / Value | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|------------------------|---------|
| 1 | Resonant (oscillation) frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ | **Hz** (for $R$ in $\Omega$, $C$ in F) | Only when $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$ |
| 2 | Angular frequency | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | **rad/sec** | $\omega_0 = 2 \pi f_0$ |
| 3 | Feedback factor at $f_0$ | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$ | Dimensionless | Peak value of the band-pass response |
| 4 | Required amplifier gain | $A = 3$ | Dimensionless | Set by $R_f = 2 R_i$ |
| 5 | Loop gain | $A \cdot \beta = 1$ | Dimensionless | **Barkhausen Criterion** |
| 6 | Closed-loop gain expression | $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ | Dimensionless | Non-inverting configuration |
| 7 | Design equation for resistors | $R_f = 2 R_i$ | $\Omega$ | To satisfy $A = 3$ |
| 8 | General β-network transfer function | $\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$ | Complex | Real part $1/3$, imaginary part varies |
| 9 | Phase angle of β-network | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left[\dfrac{1}{\omega RC} - \omega RC\right]$ | **Radians** or **Degrees** | Zero only at $\omega RC = 1$ |
| 10 | Magnitude of β-network | $\vert \beta \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{9 + \left(\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)^2}}$ | Dimensionless | Maximum = 1/3 at resonance |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Wien Bridge is the *workhorse* oscillator in:
- **Audio function generators** (20 Hz – 20 kHz) used in every KTU electronics lab.
- **Tone-detection circuits** in telecommunication and musical instrument (guitar) tuning.
- **AC measurement bridges** — the original Wien bridge was used to measure capacitance accurately.
- **Ultrasonic cleaning equipment**, biomedical stimulators, and low-frequency signal sources for transducer testing.

> [!NOTE]
> The reason this oscillator is preferred over the *RC phase-shift oscillator* in the audio range is its **superior frequency stability** and **very low harmonic distortion** (typically < 0.1% with proper amplitude stabilisation using diodes or thermistors).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transfer Function of the Wien (Lead-Lag) Network

Consider the series arm impedance $Z_s$ and the parallel arm impedance $Z_p$:

$$Z_s = R + \dfrac{1}{j \omega C} = \dfrac{1 + j \omega R C}{j \omega C}$$

$$Z_p = \dfrac{R \cdot \dfrac{1}{j \omega C}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j \omega C}} = \dfrac{R}{1 + j \omega R C}$$

Using the **voltage divider rule**, the feedback factor is the ratio of $Z_p$ to the total series-parallel impedance:

$$\beta = \dfrac{V_f}{V_{out}} = \dfrac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$

Substituting the expressions for $Z_s$ and $Z_p$:

$$\beta = \dfrac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j \omega R C}}{\dfrac{1 + j \omega R C}{j \omega C} + \dfrac{R}{1 + j \omega R C}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $j \omega C \,(1 + j \omega R C)$ to clear fractions:

$$\beta = \dfrac{j \omega R C}{(1 + j \omega R C)^2 + j \omega R C}$$

Now expand the squared term: $(1 + j \omega R C)^2 = 1 + 2 j \omega R C + (j \omega R C)^2 = 1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 + 2 j \omega R C$.

So the denominator becomes:

$$(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 + 2 j \omega R C) + j \omega R C = (1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + 3 j \omega R C$$

Therefore:

$$\beta(j \omega) = \dfrac{j \omega R C}{(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + 3 j \omega R C}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Frequency of Zero Phase Shift (Oscillation Frequency)

For the **Barkhausen criterion** to be satisfied, the total phase shift around the loop must be zero. Since the non-inverting amplifier contributes $0°$, the Wien network alone must contribute $0°$ — i.e., the **imaginary part of its denominator must vanish** when normalised.

Equivalently, the ratio $(\omega R C - \dfrac{1}{\omega R C})$ inside the phase formula must equal zero:

$$\omega R C - \dfrac{1}{\omega R C} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega R C = \dfrac{1}{\omega R C}$$

Multiplying both sides by $\omega R C$:

$$\omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega R C = 1$$

Solving for $\omega$:

$$\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{R C}$$

Converting to **cyclic frequency in Hz**:

$$f_0 = \dfrac{\omega_0}{2 \pi} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$$

> This is the **resonant (oscillation) frequency** — the *single* frequency at which the Wien oscillator naturally runs.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Feedback Factor at Resonance

Substitute $\omega = \omega_0 = 1/(RC)$ back into the transfer function:

$$\beta(j \omega_0) = \dfrac{j \,(1)}{(1 - 1^2) + 3 j \,(1)} = \dfrac{j}{0 + 3 j} = \dfrac{j}{3 j}$$

The $j$ terms cancel:

$$\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

This is a *real, positive, frequency-independent* number — exactly what is needed for the loop phase to be zero.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Required Amplifier Gain

Applying the **Barkhausen magnitude condition**:

$$\vert A \beta \vert = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A = \dfrac{1}{\beta} = \dfrac{1}{1/3} = 3$$

For the non-inverting op-amp configuration:

$$A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i} = 3 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{R_f}{R_i} = 2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_f = 2 R_i$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (frequently asked in KTU exams):** Design a Wien Bridge Oscillator to generate a sine wave of frequency $f_0 = 1 \text{ kHz}$. Assume $C = 0.01 \, \mu F$.

**Step 1** — Use $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ and solve for $R$:

$$R = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f_0 C} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 1000 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}$$

Computing denominator:

$$2 \pi \times 1000 \times 10^{-8} = 2 \pi \times 10^{-5} = 6.2832 \times 10^{-5}$$

Therefore:

$$R = \dfrac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 15{,}915 \, \Omega \approx 15.9 \, k\Omega$$

**Step 2** — Use $R_f = 2 R_i$. Choose $R_i = 10 \, k\Omega$ (a standard E12 value), then:

$$R_f = 2 \times 10 = 20 \, k\Omega$$

**Step 3** — Verify the loop gain:

$$A = 1 + \dfrac{20 k}{10 k} = 1 + 2 = 3 \quad \checkmark$$
$$\vert A \beta \vert = 3 \times \dfrac{1}{3} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Result:** With $R \approx 15.9 \, k\Omega$, $C = 0.01 \, \mu F$, $R_f = 20 \, k\Omega$, $R_i = 10 \, k\Omega$, the circuit oscillates at **exactly 1 kHz**.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Derivation | Input Given | Technique Used | Output Result |
|------------|-------------|----------------|---------------|
| β-network transfer function | Series-parallel impedances | Voltage divider + complex algebra | $\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{j\omega RC}{(1-\omega^2R^2C^2) + 3j\omega RC}$ |
| Oscillation frequency | Imaginary part = 0 | Solving $\omega RC = 1/(\omega RC)$ | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| Feedback factor | Substitute $\omega_0$ | Cancel $j$ terms | $\beta = 1/3$ |
| Gain requirement | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | Algebraic inversion | $A = 3$ |
| Resistor design | $A = 1 + R_f/R_i$ | Set $R_f = 2R_i$ | $R_f = 2R_i$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[DC Supply] --> pA1[Op-Amp IC 741]
    src2[DC Supply] --> pA1
    pA1 --> ampy[Non-Inverting Amplifier Stage]
    ampy --> outA[Output Vout to CRO]
    outA --> pB1[Series RC Arm Zs]
    pB1 --> pJ[Junction Node]
    pB1 -. feedback path .-> fbA[Feedback to Inverting Input]
    pB2[Parallel RC Arm Zp] --> pJ
    pJ --> fbB[Feedback to Non-Inverting Input]
    fbB --> ampy
    fbA --> ampy
    resR1[Resistor Ri] --> fbA
    resR2[Resistor Rf] --> fbA
    outA --> cro[CRO Display Sine Wave]
```

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Signal Flow Description

The diagram above represents the **functional flow of signals** through the Wien Bridge Oscillator. The key idea is that the output $V_{out}$ of the op-amp is **simultaneously** the signal source and the load for the RC bridge.

| Block Label | Function | Component Realisation |
|-------------|----------|------------------------|
| Non-Inverting Amplifier Stage | Provides voltage gain $A = 1 + R_f/R_i$ with $0°$ phase shift | Op-amp 741 with $R_f$ and $R_i$ |
| Series RC Arm ($Z_s$) | Forms one arm of the Wien bridge; introduces lead behaviour at high frequencies | Resistor $R_1$ in series with $C_1$ |
| Parallel RC Arm ($Z_p$) | Forms the other arm; introduces lag behaviour at low frequencies | Resistor $R_2$ in parallel with $C_2$ |
| Junction Node | Point where feedback voltage $V_f$ is tapped | Common node between the two arms |
| Feedback to (+) Input | Returns a phase-coherent fraction of the output to the non-inverting terminal | Direct wire from junction |
| Feedback to (−) Input | Returns the negative feedback through $R_f$ to control gain | Through $R_f$ and $R_i$ divider |
| CRO Display | Final measurement of the generated sine wave | Cathode Ray Oscilloscope probe |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram for KTU viva:** Trace the loop *exactly once* from the output, through $Z_s$ and $Z_p$, back into the (+) input, and through the amplifier back to the output. Count the phase contributions: **0° from amp + 0° from network at $f_0$ = 0° total**. This is the **Barkhausen phase condition**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations. List the two conditions that must be satisfied.**

**Model Answer:**
The Barkhausen criterion states that a feedback circuit will produce sustained (undamped) sinusoidal oscillations when the following **two conditions** are simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Loop gain magnitude condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must be **unity**, i.e., $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$. (1 mark)
2. **Loop phase condition:** The total phase shift around the closed loop must be **zero** (or an integer multiple of $2\pi$ radians, i.e., $360°$). (1 mark)

**Application to Wien Bridge:** At the oscillation frequency $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$, the non-inverting amplifier provides $A = 3$ with $0°$ shift, and the Wien network provides $\beta = 1/3$ with $0°$ shift, giving $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$ and total phase = $0°$. (1 mark)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Why is the feedback factor $\beta$ of the Wien network taken as $1/3$ and not any other value?**

**Model Answer:**
The feedback factor $\beta$ of the Wien lead-lag network is a *function of frequency* given by:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{j \omega R C}{(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + 3 j \omega R C}$$

Its magnitude attains a **maximum value of exactly 1/3** at the unique frequency where $\omega R C = 1$, which is $f_0 = 1/(2 \pi R C)$. (1.5 marks)

For the **Barkhausen magnitude condition** $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$ to be satisfied with a *stable* design, the amplifier must be set so that $A = 1/\beta$. At resonance, $A = 1/(1/3) = 3$. Any other value of $\beta$ would either (i) not coincide with maximum signal transfer or (ii) require an impractical gain value. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> *As per the KTU 2024 Scheme, students answer ONE full question from a choice of TWO in the ESE Module Examination.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a) With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a Wien Bridge Oscillator using an op-amp. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A Wien Bridge Oscillator consists of an op-amp (typically IC 741) in a non-inverting configuration. The non-inverting input (+) receives feedback from a Wien bridge network formed by $R_1$–$C_1$ in series and $R_2$–$C_2$ in parallel. The inverting input (−) is connected to a voltage divider $R_f$–$R_i$ that sets the closed-loop gain. The output is a sustained sine wave tapped at the op-amp output terminal.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
The op-amp amplifies the feedback signal with gain $A = 1 + R_f/R_i$ and $0°$ phase shift. The Wien network acts as a band-pass filter that allows only one frequency (the resonant frequency) to be fed back with $0°$ phase shift. When $A\beta = 1$, the loop becomes self-sustaining and produces a continuous sine wave.

**Derivation of Frequency (3 marks):**
The feedback factor of the Wien network is:

$$\beta = \dfrac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p} = \dfrac{j\omega RC}{(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + 3 j \omega R C}$$

For the phase of $\beta$ to be zero, the imaginary part of the denominator (after taking the ratio to real part) must vanish, which gives $\omega R C = 1$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\, f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C} \,}$$

assuming $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$.

---

**(b) Design a Wien Bridge Oscillator to generate a 5 kHz sine wave. Choose $C = 1 \, nF$ and find the required values of $R$ and feedback resistors.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate R from frequency formula (3 marks):**
$$R = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f_0 C} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 5000 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$R = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 5 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1}{31.416 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{\, R \approx 31.83 \, k\Omega \,}$$

(Use $R_1 = R_2 = 31.83 \, k\Omega$; nearest E12 standard value is $33 \, k\Omega$.)

**Step 2 — Choose $R_i$ and find $R_f$ (2 marks):**
Required gain $A = 3$, so $R_f = 2 R_i$. Choose $R_i = 10 \, k\Omega$ (standard).

$$R_f = 2 \times 10 \, k\Omega = 20 \, k\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Verification (2 marks):**
$$A = 1 + \dfrac{20k}{10k} = 3 \quad \checkmark$$
$$\beta = 1/3 \quad \text{(at } f_0 \text{)} \quad \checkmark$$
$$\vert A \beta \vert = 1 \quad \text{(Barkhausen satisfied)} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Design:** $R_1 = R_2 = 33 \, k\Omega$, $C_1 = C_2 = 1 \, nF$, $R_f = 20 \, k\Omega$, $R_i = 10 \, k\Omega$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a) Explain the concept of amplitude stabilisation in a Wien Bridge Oscillator. Why is it necessary, and what are the common methods used?** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Why amplitude stabilisation is necessary (3 marks):**
In a practical Wien Bridge Oscillator, if the loop gain is *exactly* 3, the circuit is theoretically balanced. However, in real components:
- Op-amp gain drifts with temperature.
- Resistor values have tolerances ($\pm 1\%$ to $\pm 5\%$).
- Supply voltage variations occur.

If $A\beta > 1$ even slightly, the output grows until the op-amp saturates, producing a **clipped (distorted) square-like wave** instead of a pure sine wave. If $A\beta < 1$, oscillations die out. Hence, an *automatic gain control (AGC)* mechanism is essential to keep $A\beta = 1$ under all conditions.

**Common Methods (4 marks):**

1. **Thermistor method (most common):** Use a *negative temperature coefficient (NTC) thermistor* in place of $R_f$. As output amplitude rises, the thermistor heats up, its resistance drops, and the gain $A = 1 + R_f/R_i$ decreases, thereby stabilising the amplitude.

2. **Diode-resistor network (incandescent lamp / back-to-back zener):** Two anti-parallel diodes (or a small incandescent bulb) are connected in parallel with $R_f$. At low amplitudes, the diodes do not conduct and gain is high; as amplitude grows, they begin to conduct, effectively reducing $R_f$ and lowering the gain.

3. **FET-based AGC:** A junction FET is used as a voltage-controlled resistor in the feedback path. A peak detector samples the output and adjusts the FET's gate voltage to control its channel resistance, automatically maintaining constant amplitude.

4. **Using a JFET as a voltage-variable resistor (VVR):** Similar principle — gain is electronically controlled by an external DC bias.

---

**(b) Compare the Wien Bridge Oscillator with the RC Phase-Shift Oscillator in terms of frequency range, distortion, stability, and component count.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Wien Bridge Oscillator | RC Phase-Shift Oscillator |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| Frequency range | **1 Hz to 1 MHz** (audio optimum) | **1 Hz to 100 kHz** (narrower) |
| Output waveform purity | Very low distortion (< 0.1% with AGC) | Higher distortion (≈ 1–2%) |
| Frequency stability | **Excellent** — RC components symmetric | Moderate — three RC sections, mismatch errors |
| Number of RC sections | **2 sections** (one series, one parallel) | **3 sections** in cascade |
| Amplifier configuration | Non-inverting (gain = 3) | Inverting (gain = −29) |
| Feedback factor | $\beta = 1/3$ (real) | $\beta = 1/29$ (real) |
| Components required | Fewer (≈ 7 active + passive) | More (≈ 8 active + passive) |
| Phase shift at $f_0$ | $0°$ from network | $180°$ from three RC sections |
| Starting of oscillations | Easy — natural Barkhausen match | Requires higher initial gain |
| Application preference | **Preferred for audio & lab generators** | Used in low-cost fixed-frequency designs |

(Allocate 0.5–1 mark per meaningful comparison row for full credit.)

**Conclusion:** The Wien Bridge Oscillator is the *superior* choice for general-purpose laboratory use due to its lower distortion and higher stability, while the RC phase-shift oscillator is reserved for cost-sensitive, single-frequency applications.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the assumption** $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$ before writing $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$ — **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 2. **Writing the gain as $A = R_f/R_i$** (the inverting amp formula) instead of $A = 1 + R_f/R_i$ (non-inverting). This is a *very common slip* and costs full marks in the design part. **−2 marks**.
> 3. **Not stating both Barkhausen conditions explicitly** (magnitude = 1 **and** phase = 0) — most students write only one. **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Plugging wrong units into the formula** — using $C$ in $\mu F$ without converting to $F$ gives an off-by-$10^6$ answer. Always write the unit conversion line. **−1 mark**.
> 5. **Skipping the verification step** $A\beta = 1$ at the end of a design problem. Examiners *love* this final check — it is worth **+1 mark** and protects against arithmetic errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ⭐ Wien Bridge Oscillator = **Op-amp + Lead-Lag RC network**.
- ⭐ Oscillation frequency: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ (only when $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$).
- ⭐ Feedback factor at resonance: $\beta = 1/3$.
- ⭐ Required amplifier gain: $A = 3$.
- ⭐ Barkhausen criteria: $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$ **AND** total phase shift = $0°$ (or $360° n$).
- ⭐ Resistor design rule: $R_f = 2 R_i$ (for non-inverting amp with gain 3).
- ⭐ Amplifier is in **non-inverting** configuration — never confuse with inverting formula.
- ⭐ RC network gives **zero phase shift at exactly one frequency** — this is the genius of the design.
- ⭐ **Amplitude stabilisation** is *mandatory* in practice; common methods are thermistor, diode-resistor, FET-AGC.
- ⭐ Real-world uses: audio signal generators, function generators, AC measurement bridges, tone detectors.
- ⭐ Advantages over RC phase-shift: lower distortion, better stability, fewer components, easier startup.
- ⭐ Typical IC used: **µA741** (or any general-purpose op-amp such as TL081, LM358).
- ⭐ Typical practical frequency range: **10 Hz to 100 kHz** with standard components.
- ⭐ Common KTU viva questions: "Why gain = 3?", "What happens if gain < 3?", "Why is the network called lead-lag?".
- ⭐ **Verification line to always include**: $A \cdot \beta = 3 \times \tfrac{1}{3} = 1$ ✓.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Astable Multivibrator Using IC 555
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRAL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Astable Multivibrator Using IC 555 ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **Astable Multivibrator** is a free-running oscillator circuit that has **no stable state**. It continuously switches between two quasi-stable states without any external triggering pulse, producing a rectangular/square wave output. When implemented using the **IC 555 timer**, the device operates in its **astable (oscillator) mode** by using two external resistors ($R_A$ and $R_B$) and one external capacitor ($C$) that determine the frequency and duty cycle of the generated waveform. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Key Definition (Must Memorize):** > *"An astable multivibrator using IC 555 is a square wave generator circuit in which the output oscillates continuously between HIGH and LOW states at a frequency determined by the RC timing network connected externally to the IC."* ### 1.2 The IC 555 Timer — Quick Overview The **555 Timer** is one of the most popular and versatile linear integrated circuits ever manufactured. It was designed by **Hans Camenzind in 1971** and is widely used for timing, pulse generation, and oscillator applications. A single 555 IC can operate in three modes: 1. **Monostable Multivibrator** (one stable state) 2. **Astable Multivibrator** (no stable state) ← *This experiment* 3. **Bistable Multivibrator** (two stable states — flip-flop) > [!IMPORTANT] > The 555 IC contains two internal comparators, an SR flip-flop, a discharge transistor, and a resistive voltage divider. The threshold and trigger voltages are internally fixed at $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ and $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ respectively. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Automatic Swing" Imagine a **person on an automatic swing** that goes back and forth without anyone pushing it: - The swing reaches a **maximum height on the right** → this is like the capacitor voltage reaching $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ (THRESHOLD). At this point, the output **switches to LOW** (like the swing reversing). - The swing reaches a **minimum height on the left** → this is like the capacitor voltage dropping to $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ (TRIGGER). At this point, the output **switches to HIGH** (like the swing reversing again). - This **never stops** automatically — it keeps going forever, just like the astable multivibrator keeps oscillating. - The **length of the ropes** (analogous to resistor $R_B$) and the **weight of the swing** (analogous to capacitor $C$) determine **how fast it swings** — this is the **frequency of oscillation**. ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Parameters > [!NOTE] > **Standard 555 IC Parameters (Industry Standard):** > - **Supply Voltage $V_{CC}$:** +4.5 V to +15 V (typically **+5 V** or **+9 V** or **+12 V**) > - **Threshold Voltage $V_{TH}$:** $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ (≈ **66.67%** of $V_{CC}$) > - **Trigger Voltage $V_{TR}$:** $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ (≈ **33.33%** of $V_{CC}$) > - **Maximum Output Current:** **200 mA** (sink/source) > - **Operating Frequency Range:** **0.1 Hz to 500 kHz** (practical astable range) > - **Output Voltage Swing:** Approximately **0 V to $V_{CC}$ - 1.7 V** (at full load) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Internal Block Diagram of IC 555 The 555 timer contains the following internal building blocks: - **A Resistive Voltage Divider:** Three equal **5 kΩ** resistors (hence the name "555") connected between $V_{CC}$ and GND. They produce reference voltages of $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ and $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$. - **Two Comparators:** - *Upper Comparator:* Compares THRESHOLD pin (pin 6) with $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$. - *Lower Comparator:* Compares TRIGGER pin (pin 2) with $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$. - **An SR Flip-Flop (Latch):** Stores the comparator outputs and drives the output stage. - **A Discharge Transistor (NPN):** Connected to pin 7 (DISCHARGE), used to quickly discharge the external timing capacitor. - **An Output Driver (Push-Pull):** Capable of sourcing/sinking up to **200 mA**. ### 2.2 Pin Configuration of IC 555 (8-Pin DIP Package) | Pin No. | Pin Name | Function | |:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | **GND** | Ground reference (0 V) | | 2 | **TRIGGER** | Starts timing when voltage falls below $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ | | 3 | **OUT** | Output pin (drives load — LED, buzzer, etc.) | | 4 | **RESET** | Active-low reset; tie to $V_{CC}$ for normal operation | | 5 | **CONTROL VOLTAGE** | Allows external modulation of threshold/trigger (decouple with 0.01 μF) | | 6 | **THRESHOLD** | Ends timing cycle when voltage rises above $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ | | 7 | **DISCHARGE** | Open-collector discharge path for timing capacitor | | 8 | **$V_{CC}$** | Positive supply voltage | ### 2.3 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step (KTU Board Favourite) **State 1 — Capacitor Charging (Output HIGH):** - The discharge transistor (Q1) is **OFF** (cut-off). - Current flows from $V_{CC}$ through $R_A$ and $R_B$ (in series) into capacitor $C$. - The capacitor charges exponentially toward $V_{CC}$ with time constant $\tau_1 = (R_A + R_B)C$. - The output remains **HIGH** until $V_C$ (capacitor voltage) reaches $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$. **State 2 — Capacitor Discharging (Output LOW):** - As soon as $V_C$ crosses $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$, the upper comparator fires → flip-flop resets. - The discharge transistor (Q1) turns **ON** (saturation). - The capacitor now discharges through $R_B$ and the discharge transistor to GND. - Time constant during discharge: $\tau_2 = R_B C$. - The output remains **LOW** until $V_C$ drops to $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$. **State 3 — Cycle Repeats:** - When $V_C$ drops to $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$, the lower comparator fires → flip-flop sets. - Discharge transistor turns **OFF** again, and the cycle restarts. - This produces a **continuous rectangular waveform** at the output (pin 3). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU BOARD EXAM** (Memorize the derivations + formulas together) | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Boundary / Note | |:---|:---:|:---|:---:|:---| | Charging Time (HIGH duration) | $t_H$ | $0.693 \times (R_A + R_B) \times C$ | seconds (s) | Time for $V_C$ to rise from $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ to $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ | | Discharging Time (LOW duration) | $t_L$ | $0.693 \times R_B \times C$ | seconds (s) | Time for $V_C$ to fall from $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ to $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ | | Total Time Period | $T$ | $0.693 \times (R_A + 2R_B) \times C$ | seconds (s) | $T = t_H + t_L$ | | Frequency of Oscillation | $f$ | $\frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \times C}$ | Hertz (Hz) | Always less than ~500 kHz | | Duty Cycle (Fractional) | $D$ | $\frac{t_H}{T} = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B}$ | dimensionless | $0.5 \le D < 1$ (always $> 50\%$) | | Duty Cycle (Percentage) | $D\%$ | $\left(\frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B}\right) \times 100$ | percent (%) | Minimum D% = 50% (when $R_A \to 0$) | | Frequency in terms of $t_H$, $t_L$ | $f$ | $\frac{1}{t_H + t_L}$ | Hz | General oscillator relationship | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** The constant $0.693$ comes from $\ln(2)$. Many students mistakenly use $0.63$ (which is $1 - 1/e$). Always use $0.693$ for astable 555 calculations. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications The 555 astable multivibrator is ubiquitous in modern electronics: - **LED Flashers and Blinker Circuits** (decorative lights, indicators) - **Tone Generators** for alarms, sirens, and doorbells - **Pulse Generation** for digital logic circuits and triggering - **Clock Signal Generators** for sequential logic and microcontroller circuits - **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** control for motor speed and LED dimming - **Missing Pulse Detectors** and **Frequency Dividers** - **Toy Circuits and Robotics** (robot eyes, sound effects) --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Charging Time ($t_H$) **Problem Setup:** During the HIGH state, the discharge transistor is OFF, and the capacitor charges from $V_{CC}$ through $R_A$ and $R_B$ in series. The charging path is: $V_{CC} \rightarrow R_A \rightarrow R_B \rightarrow C \rightarrow \text{GND}$. **Starting Equation (Universal RC Charging):** For an RC circuit charging from initial voltage $V_i$ toward final voltage $V_f$, the capacitor voltage at any time $t$ is given by: $$V_C(t) = V_f - (V_f - V_i) \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$ **Substituting the Boundary Conditions:** The initial voltage at the start of charging is $V_i = \frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ (the voltage at which the previous cycle ended). The final asymptotic voltage is $V_f = V_{CC}$ (since the capacitor is charging through both resistors to the supply). The total series resistance is $R_{\text{eq}} = R_A + R_B$. $$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - \left(V_{CC} - \frac{V_{CC}}{3}\right) \cdot e^{-t/(R_A + R_B)C}$$ Simplifying the bracket: $$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t/(R_A + R_B)C}$$ **Applying the End Condition:** The charging stops the moment $V_C(t)$ reaches the upper threshold $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$: $$\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} - \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_H/(R_A + R_B)C}$$ Rearranging — subtract $V_{CC}$ from both sides: $$-\frac{V_{CC}}{3} = -\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_H/(R_A + R_B)C}$$ Cancel $-\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ from both sides (note: $-\frac{V_{CC}}{3} \times \frac{3}{2V_{CC}} = -\frac{1}{2}$): $$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-t_H/(R_A + R_B)C}$$ Take the natural logarithm of both sides: $$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\frac{t_H}{(R_A + R_B)C}$$ Using the identity $\ln(1/2) = -\ln(2) = -0.693$: $$-0.693 = -\frac{t_H}{(R_A + R_B)C}$$ $$\boxed{t_H = 0.693 \times (R_A + R_B) \times C}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation of the Discharging Time ($t_L$) **Problem Setup:** During the LOW state, the discharge transistor is ON (saturated, behaves like a closed switch to GND). The capacitor discharges from its current voltage through $R_B$ to ground. The discharge path is: $C \rightarrow R_B \rightarrow \text{DISCHARGE pin} \rightarrow \text{GND}$. **Starting Equation (Universal RC Discharging):** For an RC circuit discharging from initial voltage $V_i$ toward $0$: $$V_C(t) = V_i \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$ **Substituting the Boundary Conditions:** The initial voltage at the start of discharging is $V_i = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ (the voltage at which charging ended). The total discharge resistance is $R_{\text{eq}} = R_B$ (only $R_B$ is in the discharge path; $R_A$ is blocked by the discharge transistor's saturation path direction). The discharge stops the moment $V_C(t)$ reaches $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$: $$\frac{V_{CC}}{3} = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_L/(R_B C)}$$ Dividing both sides by $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$: $$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-t_L/(R_B C)}$$ Take the natural logarithm: $$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\frac{t_L}{R_B C}$$ Substituting $\ln(1/2) = -0.693$: $$-0.693 = -\frac{t_L}{R_B C}$$ $$\boxed{t_L = 0.693 \times R_B \times C}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation of the Total Time Period ($T$) and Frequency ($f$) **Total Period** is simply the sum of HIGH time and LOW time: $$T = t_H + t_L$$ $$T = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C + 0.693(R_B)C$$ Factor out $0.693 \times C$: $$T = 0.693 \times C \times \left[(R_A + R_B) + R_B\right]$$ $$\boxed{T = 0.693 \times (R_A + 2R_B) \times C}$$ **Frequency** is the reciprocal of the period: $$f = \frac{1}{T}$$ $$f = \frac{1}{0.693 \times (R_A + 2R_B) \times C}$$ Since $\frac{1}{0.693} \approx 1.4431 \approx 1.44$: $$\boxed{f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \times C}}$$ ### 3.4 Derivation of the Duty Cycle ($D$) **Definition:** Duty cycle is the fraction of the total period during which the output remains HIGH: $$D = \frac{t_H}{T}$$ Substituting the formulas: $$D = \frac{0.693(R_A + R_B)C}{0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C}$$ The $0.693$ and $C$ cancel: $$\boxed{D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B}}$$ **Expressed as a percentage:** $$\boxed{D\% = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%}$$ **Important Limiting Case:** If $R_A \to 0$, then: $$D\% \to \frac{R_B}{2R_B} \times 100\% = 50\%$$ This proves that the 555 astable multivibrator **can never achieve a duty cycle below 50%** in its standard configuration. To get $D < 50\%$, a diode is typically added in parallel with $R_B$ to bypass the charging resistor. ### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style Problem) > **Example:** In a 555 astable multivibrator, $R_A = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_B = 6.8 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $C = 0.1 \mu F$. Calculate the frequency, time period, and duty cycle. Assume $V_{CC} = 12 \text{ V}$. **Step 1 — Calculate $t_H$:** $$t_H = 0.693 \times (R_A + R_B) \times C$$ $$t_H = 0.693 \times (1\text{ k}\Omega + 6.8\text{ k}\Omega) \times 0.1\,\mu F$$ $$t_H = 0.693 \times 7.8 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\boxed{t_H = 0.693 \times 7.8 \times 10^{-4} = 5.4054 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} \approx 540.54\,\mu s}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate $t_L$:** $$t_L = 0.693 \times R_B \times C$$ $$t_L = 0.693 \times 6.8 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\boxed{t_L = 0.693 \times 6.8 \times 10^{-4} = 4.7124 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} \approx 471.24\,\mu s}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate Total Period $T$:** $$T = t_H + t_L = 540.54\,\mu s + 471.24\,\mu s$$ $$\boxed{T = 1011.78\,\mu s \approx 1.012\,\text{ms}}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate Frequency $f$:** $$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{1.012 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$\boxed{f \approx 988.14\,\text{Hz} \approx 988\,\text{Hz}}$$ **Step 5 — Calculate Duty Cycle:** $$D\% = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100 = \frac{1 + 6.8}{1 + 13.6} \times 100 = \frac{7.8}{14.6} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{D\% \approx 53.42\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always show units throughout the calculation. Many students lose 1–2 marks by writing bare numbers without units ($k\Omega$, $\mu F$, $Hz$, $s$). --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 555 Timer Pinout — Mermaid Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef pinBox fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef supply fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#BF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gndBox fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 pin1[("PIN 1: GND - Ground Reference")]:::gndBox pin2[("PIN 2: TRIGGER - Input below Vcc/3")]:::pinBox pin3[("PIN 3: OUT - Output Waveform")]:::pinBox pin4[("PIN 4: RESET - Active Low Reset")]:::pinBox pin5[("PIN 5: CTRL - Control Voltage")]:::pinBox pin6[("PIN 6: THRESHOLD - Input above 2Vcc/3")]:::pinBox pin7[("PIN 7: DISCHARGE - Open Collector")]:::pinBox pin8[("PIN 8: VCC - Positive Supply 4.5 to 15 V")]:::supply pin1 --- pin2 --- pin3 --- pin4 --- pin5 --- pin6 --- pin7 --- pin8 ``` ### 4.2 555 Astable Multivibrator — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef source fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#D79B00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resistor fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ic fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cap fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#9673A6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 VCC[("VCC Supply +9V to +15V")]:::source RA[("Resistor RA - Charging Path")]:::resistor RB[("Resistor RB - Charge and Discharge")]:::resistor IC555[("IC 555 Timer Internal Block Comparators Latch Discharge Transistor")]:::ic DISC[("Pin 7 DISCHARGE Open Collector Transistor")]:::ic C[("Timing Capacitor C Connects Pin 6 and Pin 2 to GND")]:::cap THR[("Pin 6 THRESHOLD Comparator Level 2Vcc by 3")]:::ic TRG[("Pin 2 TRIGGER Comparator Level Vcc by 3")]:::ic OUT[("Pin 3 OUTPUT - Square Wave to LED or Load")]:::output GND[("GND - Common Ground Reference")]:::source VCC --> RA RA --> RB RB --> THR RB --> TRG RB --> DISC THR --> C TRG --> C C --> GND IC555 --> THR IC555 --> TRG IC555 --> DISC IC555 --> OUT DISC -.Shorts Capacitor During LOW.-> C ``` ### 4.3 Waveform Generation — State Machine Flow ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> State_HIGH: Power On State_HIGH: STATE 1 - CHARGING
Discharge Transistor OFF
Capacitor charges via RA and RB
Output is HIGH 3.3V typical State_HIGH --> State_LOW: Vc reaches 2Vcc by 3 Threshold State_LOW: STATE 2 - DISCHARGING
Discharge Transistor ON
Capacitor discharges via RB
Output is LOW 0V typical State_LOW --> State_HIGH: Vc drops to Vcc by 3 Trigger State_LOW --> State_LOW: Continuous oscillation continues State_HIGH --> State_HIGH: Cycle repeats ``` ### 4.4 Standard Circuit Schematic (ASCII Schematic View) ``` +VCC (+9V or +12V) | | [8] VCC | +--------+ | | | IC |----[4] RESET ---> +VCC (tied HIGH) | 555 | | |----[3] OUTPUT ----> To LED + Scope | | | |----[5] CTRL ---> 0.01uF ---> GND | | | |----[6] THRESH ---+ | | | | |----[7] DISC ----+---+ | | | | | |----[2] TRIG ----+ | | | | [1] GND | [RA] (e.g., 1k) | | | | | [RB] (e.g., 6.8k) | | | | +---> [C] (0.1uF) ---> GND | | +--------+ | GND (0V) ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Note:** Always label the **control voltage pin (5)** with a decoupling capacitor (typically **0.01 μF** to **0.1 μF**). The reset pin (4) must be tied to $V_{CC}$; leaving it floating causes erratic behavior. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern]** --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]** **Define an astable multivibrator. With the help of a block diagram, explain how an IC 555 can be used to generate astable oscillations.** **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** An **astable multivibrator** is a free-running oscillator circuit that has **no stable state** and continuously switches between two quasi-stable states, producing a **rectangular (square) waveform** without any external trigger. In the **astable mode of IC 555**, pins 2 (TRIGGER) and 6 (THRESHOLD) are shorted together and connected to the external timing capacitor $C$. Pins 7 and 8 are tied to $V_{CC}$ through resistors $R_A$ and $R_B$, with $R_B$ acting as the common charging/discharging element. **Operation in two phases:** - **Phase 1 (Charging):** The internal discharge transistor is OFF, and capacitor $C$ charges through $R_A + R_B$ toward $V_{CC}$. The output remains HIGH until $V_C$ reaches $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$. - **Phase 2 (Discharging):** The internal discharge transistor turns ON, and $C$ discharges through $R_B$ only. The output remains LOW until $V_C$ drops to $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$, then the cycle repeats indefinitely. > [!IMPORTANT] > **3-Mark Question Rule:** A definition (1 mark) + brief operation with 2 phases (1 mark) + key formula for frequency (1 mark). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]** **List the three operating modes of IC 555 timer. For astable mode, write the formulas for (a) charging time, (b) discharging time, and (c) frequency of oscillation.** **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **The three operating modes of IC 555 are:** 1. **Monostable Multivibrator** (one stable state — needs external trigger) 2. **Astable Multivibrator** (no stable state — free running) 3. **Bistable Multivibrator** (two stable states — acts as flip-flop) **Formulas for Astable Mode:** - **(a) Charging Time (HIGH duration):** $t_H = 0.693 \times (R_A + R_B) \times C$ - **(b) Discharging Time (LOW duration):** $t_L = 0.693 \times R_B \times C$ - **(c) Frequency of Oscillation:** $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \times C}$ > [!NOTE] > **3-Mark Question Rule:** Listing the 3 modes (1.5 marks) + writing 3 correct formulas (1.5 marks). Always include the constant **0.693** and the multiplier **1.44**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – ESE Pattern, Module Internal Choice Mandatory]** --- ### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern]** **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an astable multivibrator using IC 555. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and duty cycle. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Circuit Diagram:** The standard 555 astable circuit has the following connections: - Pin 1 → GND - Pin 2 (TRIGGER) and Pin 6 (THRESHOLD) are tied together and connected to capacitor $C$. - Pin 3 (OUT) is the output. - Pin 4 (RESET) tied to $V_{CC}$. - Pin 5 (CONTROL) decoupled to GND via 0.01 μF. - Pin 7 (DISCHARGE) connected to the junction of $R_A$ and $R_B$. - Pin 8 → $V_{CC}$. - $R_A$ connected between $V_{CC}$ and pin 7. $R_B$ connected between pin 7 and pin 2/6 junction. **Working Principle:** The internal SR flip-flop of the 555 controls the discharge transistor. The two comparators continuously monitor the capacitor voltage $V_C$ against the reference levels $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ and $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$. **Phase 1 — Charging (Output HIGH):** Initially, the discharge transistor Q1 is OFF. Capacitor $C$ charges through $R_A$ and $R_B$ in series. The output is HIGH. Charging continues until $V_C = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$, at which point the upper comparator triggers and resets the flip-flop. **Phase 2 — Discharging (Output LOW):** The flip-flop output goes HIGH at its $\bar{Q}$ terminal, turning ON the discharge transistor Q1. Capacitor $C$ now discharges through $R_B$ only (since the discharge transistor shorts pin 7 to GND). The output is LOW. Discharging continues until $V_C = \frac{V_{CC}}{3}$, at which point the lower comparator triggers and sets the flip-flop, repeating the cycle. **Derivation of Frequency:** During charging, $V_C$ rises from $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ to $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ with time constant $(R_A + R_B)C$: $$t_H = 0.693 \times (R_A + R_B) \times C$$ During discharging, $V_C$ falls from $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ to $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ with time constant $R_B C$: $$t_L = 0.693 \times R_B \times C$$ Total period: $$T = t_H + t_L = 0.693 \times (R_A + 2R_B) \times C$$ Frequency of oscillation: $$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \times C}$$ **Derivation of Duty Cycle:** $$D = \frac{t_H}{T} = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B}$$ $$D\% = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often write the duty cycle formula as $\frac{R_A}{R_A + 2R_B}$ — this is **WRONG**. The numerator must include $R_B$ as well. Remember: during charging, the capacitor charges through **both** $R_A$ AND $R_B$. --- **(b)** Design an astable multivibrator using IC 555 to generate a square wave of **frequency 1 kHz** and **duty cycle 75%**. Assume $C = 0.1 \mu F$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Frequency $f = 1 \text{ kHz} = 1000 \text{ Hz}$ - Duty cycle $D\% = 75\%$ - Capacitor $C = 0.1 \mu F = 0.1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}$ **Step 1 — Express in terms of $R_A$ and $R_B$:** $$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \times C}$$ $$D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} = 0.75$$ **Step 2 — From the duty cycle equation:** $$0.75 (R_A + 2R_B) = R_A + R_B$$ $$0.75 R_A + 1.5 R_B = R_A + R_B$$ $$0.5 R_B = 0.25 R_A$$ $$R_A = 2 R_B$$ **Step 3 — From the frequency equation:** $$1000 = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$(R_A + 2R_B) \times 10^{-7} = 1.44 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$R_A + 2R_B = 14400\,\Omega = 14.4\,\text{k}\Omega$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $R_A = 2R_B$:** $$2R_B + 2R_B = 14.4\,\text{k}\Omega$$ $$4R_B = 14.4\,\text{k}\Omega$$ $$\boxed{R_B = 3.6\,\text{k}\Omega}$$ **Step 5 — Find $R_A$:** $$R_A = 2 \times 3.6 = 7.2\,\text{k}\Omega$$ **Step 6 — Verification:** $$f = \frac{1.44}{(7.2 + 2 \times 3.6) \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.44}{14.4 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1.44}{1.44 \times 10^{-3}} = 1000\,\text{Hz} \checkmark$$ $$D\% = \frac{7.2 + 3.6}{7.2 + 7.2} \times 100 = \frac{10.8}{14.4} \times 100 = 75\% \checkmark$$ **Final Component Values:** - $R_A = 7.2 \text{ k}\Omega$ - $R_B = 3.6 \text{ k}\Omega$ - $C = 0.1 \mu F$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** Always show **substitution and verification** steps. Students who skip verification lose **1 mark** even if the final answer is correct. Also, **prefer standard resistor values** (e.g., use $R_A = 6.8$ kΩ or $7.5$ kΩ, $R_B = 3.3$ kΩ or $3.9$ kΩ) if asked for a practical design. --- ### **Question Choice B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern, Internal Choice]** **(a)** Explain the internal block diagram of IC 555 timer in detail. List all eight pins with their functions. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The IC 555 timer consists of the following internal blocks: **1. Resistive Voltage Divider:** Three equal **5 kΩ** resistors connected in series between $V_{CC}$ and GND. The midpoint voltages are: - $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ (at the **TRIGGER** comparator's non-inverting input) - $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ (at the **THRESHOLD** comparator's inverting input) **2. Upper Comparator (Threshold Comparator):** Its inverting input is tied to the voltage divider ($\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$), and its non-inverting input is pin 6 (THRESHOLD). When $V_{\text{TH}} > \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$, the comparator output goes HIGH, **resets** the flip-flop. **3. Lower Comparator (Trigger Comparator):** Its non-inverting input is tied to $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$, and its inverting input is pin 2 (TRIGGER). When $V_{\text{TR}} < \frac{V_{CC}}{3}$, the comparator output goes HIGH, **sets** the flip-flop. **4. SR Flip-Flop (Latch):** Stores the state. Its Q output drives the OUTPUT pin (pin 3), and its $\bar{Q}$ output drives the base of the discharge transistor. **5. Discharge Transistor (NPN):** Its collector is connected to pin 7 (DISCHARGE), emitter to GND, and base to $\bar{Q}$ of the flip-flop. When $\bar{Q}$ is HIGH, the transistor is ON, providing a discharge path for the external capacitor. **6. Output Driver:** A push-pull stage (totem-pole) capable of sourcing and sinking up to **200 mA**, providing the output at pin 3. **7. Reset Transistor:** Connected to pin 4 (RESET). An active-LOW signal on pin 4 resets the flip-flop and turns OFF the output. **Pin Functions Table:** | Pin | Name | Function | |:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | GND | Ground reference (0 V) | | 2 | TRIGGER | Starts timing when input falls below $V_{CC}/3$ | | 3 | OUTPUT | Output waveform (HIGH ≈ $V_{CC}$, LOW ≈ 0 V) | | 4 | RESET | Active-LOW reset; tied to $V_{CC}$ normally | | 5 | CONTROL | Voltage control of threshold; decoupled with 0.01 μF | | 6 | THRESHOLD | Ends timing cycle when input rises above $2V_{CC}/3$ | | 7 | DISCHARGE | Open-collector discharge path for the timing capacitor | | 8 | $V_{CC}$ | Positive supply (4.5 V to 15 V) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:** Many students forget the **RESET pin function** or describe pin 5 incorrectly. Pin 5 is **CONTROL VOLTAGE** — when an external voltage is applied, it **overrides** the internal $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ reference and changes the threshold level. If left unconnected, it should be **decoupled** with a capacitor to GND to prevent noise. --- **(b)** An astable multivibrator using IC 555 is designed with $R_A = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_B = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $C = 1 \mu F$. Calculate: - (i) Charging time $t_H$ - (ii) Discharging time $t_L$ - (iii) Total time period $T$ - (iv) Frequency of oscillation $f$ - (v) Duty cycle $D\%$ Assume $V_{CC} = 10 \text{ V}$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $R_A = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega = 4.7 \times 10^3\,\Omega$ - $R_B = 1 \text{ k}\Omega = 1 \times 10^3\,\Omega$ - $C = 1 \mu F = 1 \times 10^{-6}\,F$ - $V_{CC} = 10\,V$ **(i) Charging Time $t_H$:** $$t_H = 0.693 \times (R_A + R_B) \times C$$ $$t_H = 0.693 \times (4.7 + 1) \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$t_H = 0.693 \times 5.7 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\boxed{t_H = 3.9501 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 3.95\,\text{ms}}$$ **(ii) Discharging Time $t_L$:** $$t_L = 0.693 \times R_B \times C$$ $$t_L = 0.693 \times 1 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\boxed{t_L = 6.93 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = 0.693\,\text{ms}}$$ **(iii) Total Time Period $T$:** $$T = t_H + t_L$$ $$T = 3.95\,\text{ms} + 0.693\,\text{ms}$$ $$T = 4.643\,\text{ms} = 0.693 \times (R_A + 2R_B) \times C = 0.693 \times 6.7 \times 10^{-3} = 4.6431\,\text{ms}$$ $$\boxed{T \approx 4.643\,\text{ms}}$$ **(iv) Frequency $f$:** $$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{4.643 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$\boxed{f \approx 215.38\,\text{Hz}}$$ **(v) Duty Cycle $D\%$:** $$D\% = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100$$ $$D\% = \frac{4.7 + 1}{4.7 + 2} \times 100 = \frac{5.7}{6.7} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{D\% \approx 85.07\%}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #4:** Common student mistakes in numerical problems: > 1. Using $\frac{R_A}{R_A + 2R_B}$ instead of $\frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B}$ for duty cycle. > 2. Forgetting to convert kΩ and μF to base units (Ω and F) before multiplication. > 3. Using $0.63$ instead of $0.693$ as the constant. > 4. Missing the **units** in the final answer (mark deduction: **0.5 to 1 mark**). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔑 RAPID REVISION — MEMORIZE BEFORE EXAM** - ✅ The 555 IC has **8 pins** in a DIP package. Pin 1 = GND, Pin 8 = $V_{CC}$. - ✅ In astable mode, **pin 2 and pin 6 are tied together** and connected to capacitor $C$. - ✅ **Pin 7 (DISCHARGE)** sits between $R_A$ and $R_B$ — it is the open-collector discharge path. - ✅ **Pin 4 (RESET)** must be tied to $V_{CC}$ for normal operation; tying it LOW resets the output. - ✅ **Pin 5 (CONTROL)** should be decoupled to GND with a **0.01 μF** capacitor to prevent noise. - ✅ Internal reference voltages: $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ (trigger) and $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ (threshold). - ✅ **Charging path** is through $R_A + R_B$ in series (both resistors). - ✅ **Discharging path** is through $R_B$ only (not $R_A$). - ✅ The constant **0.693** comes from $\ln(2) \approx 0.6931$. - ✅ The constant **1.44** is approximately $\frac{1}{0.693}$. - ✅ Frequency formula: $f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \times C}$. - ✅ Duty cycle formula: $D\% = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100$. - ✅ **Duty cycle is always > 50%** in the standard 555 astable configuration. - ✅ To get duty cycle < 50%, add a **diode across $R_B$** (cathode at pin 7, anode at pin 6). - ✅ Maximum practical frequency: ~**500 kHz** for astable operation. - ✅ Maximum output current: **200 mA** (sourcing/sinking). - ✅ The 555 is often called the **"Time Machine"** IC due to its versatility. - ✅ Astable output is a **rectangular wave**, not a pure sine wave. - ✅ The output swings between approximately **0 V and $V_{CC}$** (with small drop due to internal transistors). - ✅ **Common applications:** LED flashers, tone generators, PWM modulators, clock generators, alarms.

topics.title: Monostable Multivibrator Using IC 555
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Monostable Multivibrator Using IC 555

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Monostable Multivibrator** (also called a *one-shot* or *mono*) is a sequential logic element possessing **one stable state** and one **quasi-stable (temporary) state**. In the stable state, the output remains at a fixed logic level indefinitely until an external *trigger pulse* is applied. Upon triggering, the circuit flips into the quasi-stable state for a precisely timed duration $T$, after which it automatically returns to its original stable state — requiring no further external input.

When implemented using the **IC 555 timer** (a classic 8-pin analog/digital hybrid IC designed by Hans Camenzind in 1972), the monostable configuration produces a single, fixed-width output pulse for every trigger edge received, making it a "single-shot pulse generator."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 OBE framework, students are expected to *design, build, and characterize* the 555-based monostable, plot the input-output waveforms, and **verify the timing equation** $T = 1.1 \, R_1 C_1$ experimentally.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the monostable like a **staircase light switch with a timer** in an apartment hallway:

- **Stable State (Idle):** The light is **OFF**. The light stays OFF forever — no matter how long you wait. This is the "rest" position.
- **Trigger (Press the switch):** Someone walks by and presses the switch momentarily.
- **Quasi-stable State (Timer Active):** The light immediately turns **ON**, and an internal timer starts.
- **Auto-Return:** After a fixed delay (say, 60 seconds), the light **automatically switches OFF** — even if no one is around — and waits for the next press.

The **IC 555 in monostable mode behaves identically**: an incoming trigger pulse causes the output to go HIGH for a duration $T$ set by an external resistor $R$ and capacitor $C$, and then automatically return LOW — ready for the next trigger.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The IC 555 monostable is characterized by the following standard specifications used in KTU lab manuals:

- **Supply Voltage $V_{CC}$:** Standard **+5 V to +15 V** (typical $V_{CC} = +5\,\text{V}$ in digital logic labs).
- **Trigger Pulse Width:** Must be **less than** the output pulse width $T$ (otherwise re-triggering occurs).
- **Trigger Level:** Trigger occurs when pin 2 falls below **$\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$** (the lower comparator threshold).
- **Threshold Level:** Reset occurs when pin 6 rises above **$\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$** (the upper comparator threshold).
- **Output Pulse Width Range:** Practically, $10\,\mu\text{s} \le T \le \text{several minutes}$ (depending on $R$ and $C$ values).

> [!NOTE]
> The two magic fractions — **1/3** and **2/3** of $V_{CC}$ — are hardwired inside the 555's internal **resistor divider** of three equal $5\,\text{k}\Omega$ resistors, which is precisely where the IC gets its name "**555**".

### 1.4 Aim of the KTU Lab Experiment

> **Aim:** To design and set up a monostable multivibrator using the IC 555 timer, observe the input trigger and output pulse waveforms on a CRO/DSO, and verify the theoretical pulse width $T = 1.1 \, R_1 C_1$ against the measured value.

### 1.5 Apparatus Required (KTU Standard List)

- IC 555 timer (1 no.)
- Resistor $R_1$ (typically $10\,\text{k}\Omega$)
- Capacitor $C_1$ (typically $0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ or $1\,\mu\text{F}$)
- DC power supply: **+5 V / +12 V**
- Function generator (for trigger pulses)
- CRO / DSO (Cathode Ray Oscilloscope / Digital Storage Oscilloscope)
- Connecting wires and breadboard
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The IC 555 — Block-Level Architecture

The 555 is internally composed of **five functional blocks**:

1. **Resistor Divider Network:** Three equal $5\,\text{k}\Omega$ resistors setting reference voltages at $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$.
2. **Upper Comparator (Threshold Comparator):** Compares pin 6 voltage with $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$.
3. **Lower Comparator (Trigger Comparator):** Compares pin 2 voltage with $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$.
4. **SR Flip-Flop (Latch):** Stores the state of the comparators.
5. **Discharge Transistor** (open-collector NPN at pin 7) and **Output Driver Stage** (push-pull at pin 3).

### 2.2 Pin Configuration of IC 555 (DIP-8 Package)

| Pin No. | Pin Name        | Function in Monostable Mode                                                |
|:-------:|:----------------|:---------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1       | **GND**         | Ground reference (0 V)                                                     |
| 2       | **TRIG**        | Trigger input — active LOW; pulse must drop below $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$     |
| 3       | **OUT**         | Output — produces the one-shot HIGH pulse                                  |
| 4       | **RESET**       | Active LOW reset; tie to $V_{CC}$ if unused                                |
| 5       | **CTRL**        | Control voltage; tie to ground via $0.01\,\mu\text{F}$ for noise immunity |
| 6       | **THRESH**      | Threshold input — senses capacitor voltage; trips at $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$  |
| 7       | **DISCH**       | Discharge — open-collector NPN to discharge $C$ when output is LOW        |
| 8       | **$V_{CC}$**    | Positive supply (typically +5 V)                                           |

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

The monostable operation can be broken into **four distinct phases** that examiners love to test:

**Phase 1 — Stable (Idle) State**
- Initially, the SR flip-flop is RESET, so:
  - Output (pin 3) = **LOW (0 V)**
  - Internal discharge transistor (pin 7) = **ON (saturated)**
- Since pin 7 is connected to the timing capacitor $C_1$, the transistor ON state keeps $C_1$ **shorted to ground**.
- Voltage across $C_1$, $V_C = 0\,\text{V}$ (well below $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$).
- Circuit waits indefinitely in this state — this is the **stable state**.

**Phase 2 — Trigger Event**
- A **negative-going trigger pulse** is applied to pin 2.
- The moment $V_{\text{TRIG}} < \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$, the lower comparator output goes HIGH, which **SETs** the SR flip-flop.
- Consequences:
  - Output (pin 3) → **HIGH (≈ $V_{CC}$)**
  - Discharge transistor (pin 7) → **OFF**
- The timing capacitor $C_1$ is now **free to charge** through resistor $R_1$ from $V_{CC}$.

**Phase 3 — Quasi-Stable (Timing) State**
- With pin 7 OFF, capacitor $C_1$ charges exponentially towards $V_{CC}$ through $R_1$.
- The voltage across $C_1$ rises as:
  $$V_C(t) = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-t/R_1 C_1}\right)$$
- The circuit remains in this HIGH-output state **as long as** $V_C < \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$.

**Phase 4 — Auto-Reset (End of Pulse)**
- The instant $V_C$ reaches $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$, the upper comparator trips, which **RESETs** the SR flip-flop.
- Consequences:
  - Output (pin 3) → **LOW**
  - Discharge transistor (pin 7) → **ON**, rapidly discharging $C_1$ back to 0 V.
- The circuit returns to the stable state and waits for the next trigger.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|:---------|:--------|:--------------------|
| **Output Pulse Width** $T$ | $T = 1.1 \cdot R_1 C_1$ | Seconds; with $R_1$ in $\Omega$ and $C_1$ in F |
| **Lower Threshold** | $V_{\text{TRIG}} = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ | Trigger fires when pin 2 drops below this |
| **Upper Threshold** | $V_{\text{TH}} = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ | Output terminates when pin 6 rises above this |
| **Capacitor Charging Law** | $V_C(t) = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Where $\tau = R_1 C_1$ |
| **Duty Cycle** (Pulse Repetition) | $\text{Duty} = \frac{T}{T_{\text{period}}} \times 100\%$ | For triggering rate analysis |
| **Output HIGH Voltage** | $V_{\text{OH}} \approx V_{CC}$ | When sourcing current (push-pull stage) |
| **Output LOW Voltage** | $V_{\text{OL}} \approx 0\,\text{V}$ | Saturated low output |
| **Trigger Pulse Width** | $t_{\text{TRIG}} < T$ | To avoid re-triggering confusion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor **1.1** in $T = 1.1 R_1 C_1$ comes from $\ln 3 \approx 1.0986 \approx 1.1$. This is derived when the capacitor voltage reaches $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ starting from 0 V. **Memorize this derivation — KTU frequently asks it for 7 marks.**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

The 555 monostable is one of the most ubiquitous analog ICs ever made (billions produced annually). Its real-world uses include:

- **Pulse Stretching:** Converting short button-presses into defined, longer pulses for debouncing in digital systems.
- **Missing Pulse Detection:** In automotive and industrial systems, a monostable retriggered periodically — if a pulse fails to arrive, the output goes LOW and triggers an alarm.
- **Time-Delay Generation:** Power-on delays, relay activation delays, lamp fade-in timers.
- **Frequency Division:** By triggering on every $N$-th edge of a periodic input.
- **Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM) Generation:** When the input modulating signal slightly shifts the threshold.
- **Tachometers and Ratemeters:** Automotive engine RPM measurement circuits.
- **Touch-Activated Switches:** A monostable can be triggered by touch sensors to give a timed "ON" interval for LED/lamp.

### 2.6 Why "Mono" Stable?

The Greek prefix **mono-** means "one". So **monostable** = "one stable state" — in stark contrast to:
- **Astable** (no stable state; free-running oscillator)
- **Bistable** (two stable states; equivalent to a flip-flop)

This naming convention is heavily tested in KTU viva voce and Part A questions.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Timing Equation $T = 1.1 R_1 C_1$

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU exams. We must derive the exact time taken for $V_C$ to charge from 0 V to $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ through resistor $R_1$.

**Given:**
- Capacitor $C_1$ initially at $V_C(0) = 0\,\text{V}$ (discharged in the stable state).
- Charging supply = $V_{CC}$ through resistor $R_1$.
- Switching threshold = $V_C(T) = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ (when the output returns LOW).
- Time constant of RC charging: $\tau = R_1 C_1$.

**Step 1: Write the general charging equation for an RC circuit.**

A capacitor charging from 0 V toward $V_{CC}$ through resistor $R$ follows the classic first-order response:

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

This equation states that at $t = 0$, $V_C = 0$ (correct — capacitor is initially discharged), and as $t \to \infty$, $V_C \to V_{CC}$ (capacitor fully charged).

**Step 2: Substitute the boundary condition at $t = T$ (end of pulse).**

At the exact moment the output returns LOW, the capacitor voltage must equal the upper threshold:

$$V_C(T) = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$$

Plug this into the charging equation:

$$\frac{2}{3} V_{CC} = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-T/\tau}\right)$$

**Step 3: Cancel $V_{CC}$ from both sides.**

Since $V_{CC} \neq 0$, we can safely divide both sides by $V_{CC}$:

$$\frac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-T/\tau}$$

**Step 4: Isolate the exponential term.**

Rearranging:

$$e^{-T/\tau} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 5: Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

Recall that $\ln(e^x) = x$. Applying $\ln(\cdot)$ to both sides:

$$-\frac{T}{\tau} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -\ln 3$$

**Step 6: Solve for $T$.**

The two negatives cancel:

$$T = \tau \cdot \ln 3 = R_1 C_1 \cdot \ln 3$$

**Step 7: Evaluate $\ln 3$ numerically.**

$$\ln 3 \approx 1.0986 \approx 1.1$$

**Step 8: State the final result.**

$$\boxed{\,T = 1.1 \, R_1 C_1\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check for typical KTU lab values:**
> If $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C_1 = 1\,\mu\text{F}$:
> $$T = 1.1 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} = 11 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 11\,\text{ms}$$
> This pulse width is easily visible on a standard CRO.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Design a 555 monostable to produce an output pulse of width **$T = 2.2\,\text{ms}$**. Choose $C_1 = 1\,\mu\text{F}$ and find $R_1$. Then verify the threshold voltages if $V_{CC} = 12\,\text{V}$.

**Solution:**

**Part (i): Finding $R_1$**

Using $T = 1.1 R_1 C_1$:

$$R_1 = \frac{T}{1.1 \, C_1} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_1 = \frac{2.2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 2000\,\Omega = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Part (ii): Finding threshold voltages**

Lower trigger threshold (pin 2):

$$V_{\text{TRIG}} = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC} = \frac{1}{3} \times 12 = 4\,\text{V}$$

Upper threshold (pin 6):

$$V_{\text{TH}} = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} = \frac{2}{3} \times 12 = 8\,\text{V}$$

**Conclusion:** When pin 2 receives a pulse below 4 V, the output goes HIGH. After 2.2 ms, the capacitor reaches 8 V, and the output returns LOW.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Charging Phase Behavior

The following matrix maps the **time-domain behavior** of the 555 monostable — a high-yield exam reference:

| Time $t$ (in units of $\tau$) | Capacitor Voltage $V_C$ | Output State (Pin 3) | Discharge Transistor (Pin 7) | Phase |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $t = 0^-$ (just before trigger) | $0\,\text{V}$ | LOW | ON | Stable |
| $t = 0^+$ (just after trigger) | $0\,\text{V}$ | HIGH | OFF | Quasi-stable begins |
| $t = 0.1\,\tau$ | $\approx 0.095 V_{CC}$ | HIGH | OFF | Charging |
| $t = 0.5\,\tau$ | $\approx 0.393 V_{CC}$ | HIGH | OFF | Charging |
| $t = 0.693\,\tau$ | $\approx 0.5 V_{CC}$ | HIGH | OFF | Mid-point |
| $t = 1.0986\,\tau$ (i.e. $T = 1.1 R_1 C_1$) | $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ | LOW (just flipped) | ON (just turned on) | End of pulse |
| $t > T$ | $0\,\text{V}$ (discharged via pin 7) | LOW | ON | Stable (waiting) |

### 3.4 Design Constraint Analysis

For a robust KTU lab design, the following **boundary conditions** must be respected:

- $R_1$ should be chosen in the range $1\,\text{k}\Omega \le R_1 \le 1\,\text{M}\Omega$. Values below 1 kΩ stress the discharge transistor; values above 1 MΩ cause leakage-current errors.
- $C_1$ should be in the range $100\,\text{pF} \le C_1 \le 1000\,\mu\text{F}$. Below 100 pF, stray capacitance dominates; above 1000 μF, electrolytic leakage becomes significant.
- The trigger pulse width $t_{\text{TRIG}}$ should be **strictly less than** the output pulse width $T$. Otherwise, the output remains HIGH continuously (the trigger pulse is "stretched" — a useful property called **pulse-stretching monostable**).

### 3.5 Why the Output Returns LOW on Its Own — The "How" Behind It

This is conceptually subtle. The auto-reset mechanism relies on **negative feedback** through the capacitor and the two comparators. When the output is HIGH:
- Pin 7 is OFF, so $C_1$ charges.
- The instant $V_C$ reaches $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$, the upper comparator fires, which RESETS the flip-flop.
- This is an **automatic causal chain** — no external signal is required to end the pulse. This is what makes the monostable "self-terminating".

This contrasts with a **bistable** circuit, which requires an explicit external RESET pulse.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — 555 Monostable State Machine

The following Mermaid state diagram captures the **two-state behavior** of the 555 monostable, including the trigger and timeout transitions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateA : Power ON

    stateA : STABLE STATE
    stateA : Output Pin3 = LOW (0V)
    stateA : Pin7 Discharge Transistor = ON
    stateA : Capacitor C1 Voltage = 0V
    stateA : Circuit waits indefinitely

    stateB : QUASI STABLE STATE
    stateB : Output Pin3 = HIGH (approx VCC)
    stateB : Pin7 Discharge Transistor = OFF
    stateB : Capacitor C1 Charging through R1
    stateB : Vc rises exponentially toward VCC

    stateA --> stateB : Negative trigger pulse on Pin2
    stateA --> stateB : Vtrig drops below one third VCC

    stateB --> stateA : Vc reaches two thirds VCC
    stateB --> stateA : Auto timeout after T equals 1.1 R1 C1
    stateB --> stateA : Pin6 upper comparator trips

    stateA --> stateA : No trigger applied remain idle
    stateB --> stateB : Trigger held LOW extends HIGH output
```

### 4.2 Circuit Schematic — Block-Level Functional Architecture

Since drawing a full physical circuit schematic in Mermaid is impractical, the following **functional flow architecture** maps every node and signal of the 555 monostable circuit, including the external component connections.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DC SUPPLY VCC PLUS 5V TO PLUS 15V"] --> B["PIN 8 VCC OF IC 555"]
    A --> C["PIN 4 RESET TIED HIGH TO VCC"]
    D["GROUND 0V"] --> E["PIN 1 GND OF IC 555"]
    D --> F["PIN 5 CTRL DECOUPLING 0.01 MICROFARAD TO GND"]
    G["FUNCTION GENERATOR NEGATIVE TRIGGER PULSE"] --> H["PIN 2 TRIG INPUT"]
    H --> I["LOWER COMPARATOR REFERENCE ONE THIRD VCC"]
    I --> J["SR FLIP FLOP SET INPUT"]
    J --> K["OUTPUT DRIVER PUSH PULL STAGE"]
    K --> L["PIN 3 OUT TO LOAD OR CRO CHANNEL"]
    M["RESISTOR R1 BETWEEN VCC AND PIN 7"] --> N["PIN 7 DISCH OPEN COLLECTOR NPN"]
    N --> O["CAPACITOR C1 BETWEEN PIN 7 AND GND"]
    O --> P["PIN 6 THRESH INPUT SENSES Vc"]
    P --> Q["UPPER COMPARATOR REFERENCE TWO THIRDS VCC"]
    Q --> R["SR FLIP FLOP RESET INPUT"]
    S["INTERNAL 5KILO OHM RESISTOR DIVIDER"] --> I
    S --> Q
    J --> N
    R --> N
```

### 4.3 Waveform Timing Diagram (Conceptual)

The following conceptual chart visualizes the **input trigger**, **capacitor voltage**, and **output pulse** timing relationships. Since Mermaid cannot directly render CRO waveforms, we use a **functional timing state flow**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1["T ZERO Trigger Arrives"] --> step2["T ZERO PLUS Output Goes HIGH"]
    step2 --> step3["Capacitor Charges through R1"]
    step3 --> step4["Vc rises from 0V to two thirds VCC"]
    step4 --> step5["T EQUALS 1.1 R1 C1 Threshold Reached"]
    step5 --> step6["Upper Comparator Trips"]
    step6 --> step7["Flip Flop Resets"]
    step7 --> step8["Output Returns LOW"]
    step8 --> step9["Discharge Transistor ON C1 Discharges Fast"]
    step9 --> step10["Ready for Next Trigger"]
    step10 -.-> step1
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow Summary Table

| Signal / Node | Direction | Voltage Range | Timing Role |
|:--------------|:---------:|:-------------:|:------------|
| Trigger (Pin 2) | Input | Falls below $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ | Initiates quasi-stable state |
| Threshold (Pin 6) | Input (senses $V_C$) | Rises to $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ | Terminates quasi-stable state |
| Output (Pin 3) | Output | 0 V (LOW) to $V_{CC}$ (HIGH) | The one-shot pulse |
| Discharge (Pin 7) | Output (open-collector) | Floating (HIGH-Z) or 0 V | Discharges $C_1$ in stable state |
| Capacitor Voltage $V_C$ | Internal node | 0 V to $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ | Time-determining element |

### 4.5 Component Roles — Quick Architecture Map

| External Component | Connection | Function |
|:-------------------|:-----------|:---------|
| $R_1$ | Between $V_{CC}$ and Pin 7 | Sets charging time constant $\tau = R_1 C_1$ |
| $C_1$ | Between Pin 7 and GND | Stores the timing charge; voltage sampled at Pin 6 |
| $0.01\,\mu\text{F}$ | Pin 5 to GND | Bypass for control voltage — prevents noise injection |
| Pull-up at Pin 4 | Pin 4 to $V_{CC}$ | Prevents accidental reset; tie HIGH always |
| Trigger source | Function generator to Pin 2 | Provides the negative-edge trigger event |
